ai.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields make the electricity in your wall; the whole answer descends from one twitch of a meter needle in 1831. Read this in one sitting — Faraday's bench to the 500 kV lines leaving the Latrobe Valley — nothing solved, only followed.
ai.1: Some winter evening this year, Melbourne will switch itself on — kettles, trams, footy training under lights, and you, flicking a switch without a thought. Follow the wire behind that switch backwards: through the wall, under the street, through a suburban substation to a terminal station on the city's edge, then along the 500 kV lines that stride east across Gippsland. About 170 km from your kitchen, the wire ends at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
ai.2: The machine is a coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. That is the whole secret. In the Valley, steam raised by burning brown coal does the spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the windy plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Strip away the boilers and blades and almost every large generator on Earth is the same device: a coil of wire, turning between magnets.
ai.3: This Area of Study — its official title is How are fields used in electricity generation? — explains that one device and the network built around it. And it begins exactly where the last page left off: with the observation that a field can be static or changing, and that the changing kind is the kind that pays.
ai.4: Connects The last page flagged static versus changing as the consequential field classification. This Area of Study spends that idea on the grid; Unit 4 spends it again, on light itself.
ai.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday — the bookbinder's apprentice who gave us the field — had been circling one conviction for a decade. An electric current creates a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown that in 1820. Surely, then, a magnetic field should create a current. Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — connected to the second, waiting for the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
ai.6: The needle gave him nothing. Or almost nothing: at the instant he connected the battery, it twitched, then fell back to zero and stayed there, however strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant he disconnected, it twitched again — the other way. The effect he had planned for was absent; the one he had not lived entirely at the edges, in the two brief moments when the magnetic field was changing.
ai.7: By October he had the clean version of the experiment. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle kicks. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as strong a magnet as you like — and nothing flows. Pull it out and the needle kicks the opposite way.
ai.8: Motion in, current one way; motion out, current the other; no motion, no current. The field does not drive charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
ai.9: The push that a changing field gives to charges is called an induced emf — short for electromotive force, a misleading old name: it is not a force, and it is measured in volts. The phenomenon as a whole is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page commits Faraday's accident deliberately, over and over. But "the field changes" is not precise enough to build a grid on. Exactly which quantity has to change?
ai.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full set of rules for what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday's law.
ai.11: Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity Faraday's discovery hinges on is the magnetic flux: how much field passes through the window. Hold the window square-on to the field and the flux is at its greatest; tilt it and less passes through; turn it edge-on and the lines slide past without entering at all — zero flux. The formula is named ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — field strength multiplied by the area held perpendicular to it — and flux earns its own unit, the weber (Wb).
ai.12: If induction needs changing flux, the formula is a list of handles. You can change B, by strengthening or weakening the field or moving the loop nearer the magnet or further away. You can change A, the area sitting inside the field, by sliding the loop in or out. Or you can change the angle, by rotating the loop so it shows the field its face, then its edge, then its face again — three handles: field, area, angle.
ai.13: Faraday's law says the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Read it as a sentence rather than solving it: more flux change in less time means a bigger push, and a coil of N turns is pushed N times over. The size of the flux is irrelevant; only its rate of change counts. The strongest field in the world, held steady, induces precisely nothing.
ai.14: Of the three handles, the grid grips the third. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from maximum, through zero, to maximum the other way, and back — change that never runs out. Hold that picture: it is the rest of this story, with one sunlit exception at the end.
ai.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
ai.16: Faraday's law arrives wearing a minus sign, and the minus sign has its own name: Lenz's law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change creating it. Push a magnet's north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so that the coil presents a north pole back, resisting the approach; pull the magnet away and the current reverses, now pulling the magnet back. Note the precise wording — the current opposes the change in flux, never the field itself.
ai.17: To see why it must oppose, run the alternative. If the induced current aided the change, a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, pulling harder still — a runaway spiral handing out energy for free, started by a fingertip. Energy conservation forbids it, and that is all Lenz's law is: energy conservation wearing a disguise. Every joule of electrical energy you induce must be paid for as work done against the opposition.
ai.18: This is why a generator resists being turned. Spin one connected to nothing and it turns freely; connect a lamp and the crank stiffens — you can feel, in your wrist, the energy the lamp is spending. Connect a suburb and it takes a power station's worth of steam to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car runs the bargain in reverse: its motor acts as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force — banking energy in the battery instead of burning it in the brake pads.
ai.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz's law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping you met in Position is stored energy.
ai.20: Now revisit a machine you already own. Area of Study 2 built the DC motor: a coil on an axle between magnet poles; current in, the two sides of the coil pushed in opposite directions, rotation out — with a split-ring commutator reversing the current every half-turn so that the rotation never changes direction.
ai.21: Unhook the battery and spin the coil yourself. Rotation changes the flux through the coil; changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The same hardware is now a generator — the film of the motor run backwards: a motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram survives unchanged; only the energy flow turns around.
ai.22: One design choice remains — how the spinning coil hands its current to the stationary outside world — and it decides everything about the output. Slip rings, two complete rings with one joined to each end of the coil, keep continuous contact and deliver the emf exactly as it is made: reversing direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is called an alternator. The motor's own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it happens, so the outside current always flows one way — direct current, DC — rising and falling in bumps, but never reversing — one small fitting, two different products.
ai.23: Simulation 1.1 puts the crank in your hand.
ai.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
ai.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf traces the smoothest curve in physics: a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia's grid is tuned so that the swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size is quoted as 230 V, but that number hides a subtlety: the voltage at your wall actually swings between about +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That honest average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 unpacks the name.
ai.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and that wall, though, stands a physics problem. Wire has resistance, and current flowing through resistance turns precious electrical energy into useless heat — a loss named Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what is squared: the current.
ai.27: Power is voltage multiplied by current, so the same power can travel as a low voltage with a large current, or as an enormous voltage with a tiny current. Raise the voltage ten times and the current falls to a tenth — and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is not a preference; it is the entire game.
ai.28: The machine that makes voltage adjustable is Faraday's iron ring grown up: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core; nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries it through the secondary coil; Faraday's law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor, the power ideally untouched.
ai.29: Here, too, is why the grid runs on AC at all: a transformer works only with changing flux. Give it steady DC and the flux freezes — nothing is induced, and nothing comes out.
ai.30: So the journey in Figure 1.1 is Faraday's law applied over and over. A coil in the Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the long march to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a final transformer near your house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second, ready for the flick of a switch.
ai.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam's hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
ai.32: One generator on the grid breaks the spinning-coil template entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that sit silent and motionless in the sun while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no rotation: nothing moves but electrons and light.
ai.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct slightly differently. Arriving light knocks electrons loose, and an electric field, built permanently into the junction between the two slices, lets the loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way push makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field is still doing all the work — the electric field this time, standing at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
ai.34: Your house and the grid speak AC, so every rooftop system includes one more box: the inverter, which converts the panels' DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how — its electronic circuitry, like the fine detail of the silicon sandwich, is officially beyond the course.
ai.35: One question is left deliberately hanging: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? The answer needs a new idea about what light is — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening move of Unit 4, where the packets get their name: photons.
ai.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4's photoelectric effect.
ai.37: You have now read Area of Study 3 once, end to end. Pass 2 retells it as core, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
ai.38: Pass 3 is for exam season: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions worked in full. The Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam's 120 marks on every recent paper, and its favourite questions are loyal; you will meet them all there.
ai.39: Connects Begin Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction whenever you are ready, and bookmark Pass 3 · Generation of electricity for when the exam is real.

socratic.0:
Socrates. When you flick a switch tonight and a lamp lights, where would you say that electricity is born?
Student. Somewhere far off, I suppose — a power station.
Socrates. And the whole of this enquiry — how fields make the electricity in your walls — descends from one small event: a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. Shall we follow the wire from Faraday's bench all the way to the 500 kV lines leaving the Latrobe Valley? We shall solve nothing yet; we shall only trace it.
socratic.1:
Student. Then let us trace it backwards. It is a winter evening, Melbourne is drawing its load — kettles, trams, lighting, every domestic switch. Behind my own switch, where does the wire go?
Socrates. Follow it through the wall, beneath the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city's edge, then out along the 500 kV lines running east across Gippsland. How far do you think you have travelled before the wire ends?
Student. Surely many kilometres.
Socrates. About 170 of them — from your kitchen to a single machine in the Latrobe Valley.
socratic.2:
Student. What is this machine that sits at the far end?
Socrates. A coil of wire, turning in a magnetic field. Now, in the Latrobe Valley what makes it turn?
Student. Steam, I'd guess, raised by burning brown coal.
Socrates. Just so. And in the Snowy Mountains it is falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Yet set the boilers and the blades aside — what is left in every large generator?
Student. The same thing each time: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
socratic.3:
Socrates. That is what this Area of Study, How are fields used in electricity generation?, sets out to explain — that device, and the whole network built around it. Do you recall where the previous page left us?
Student. With the idea that a field can be either static or changing.
Socrates. And which of the two, do you suppose, makes electricity?
Student. If the whole story begins here, it must be the changing kind.
socratic.4:
Connects Socrates. So the distinction the last page drew — static versus changing — was the one that mattered?
Student. Yes; here we apply it to the grid, and I gather Unit 4 will apply it once more — to light itself.
socratic.5:
Socrates. Let us go back to the bench. London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder's apprentice, and the man who first conceived the field — had clung to one conviction for ten years. Oersted had shown in 1820 that an electric current creates a magnetic field. What would you expect Faraday to suspect by symmetry?
Student. That a magnetic field, in turn, ought to create a current.
Socrates. So he wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — on the second. What was he expecting it to show?
Student. A steady current in the second coil, mirroring the first.
socratic.6:
Socrates. And the needle?
Student. Tell me — did it swing across?
Socrates. Almost nothing. At the instant the battery was connected, it deflected, then fell back to zero and stayed there — however strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant of disconnection, it deflected again, the opposite way. What does that pattern tell you about when the meter responds?
Student. The effect he predicted never came. The needle only moved in those two brief moments — and those were exactly the moments when the magnetic field was changing.
socratic.7:
Socrates. By October he had pared the experiment to its plainest form. Move a bar magnet into a coil of wire — what happens?
Student. The needle deflects.
Socrates. Hold the magnet still inside the coil, for as long as you like, with the strongest magnet you can find?
Student. Then I would expect nothing at all — and you are telling me there is nothing. No current.
Socrates. And withdraw it again?
Student. The needle deflects the other way.
socratic.8:
Socrates. Gather the three results, then. Inward?
Student. Current one way.
Socrates. Outward?
Student. Current the other way.
Socrates. And no motion?
Student. No current. So it is not the field itself that drives charge through the wire — it is the change in the field.
socratic.9:
Socrates. Good. The action a changing field exerts on charges is called an induced emf — short for electromotive force. But beware the name: is it a force?
Student. You're warning me it isn't. And it's measured in volts, not newtons.
Socrates. A misleading historical label, exactly. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page reproduces Faraday's effect on purpose, over and over. But to say merely that "the field changes" — is that precise enough to build a grid upon?
Student. No. I'd want to know which quantity, precisely, must change.
socratic.10:
Connects Socrates. That full set of rules — magnet and coil, what registers on the meter and what does not — is where Pass 2 begins, isn't it?
Student. Yes, with Faraday's law.
socratic.11:
Socrates. So let us name the quantity. Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the field lines as threads passing through the opening. How much field gets through — what shall we call that?
Student. Some measure of how much field passes through the loop.
Socrates. We call it the magnetic flux. When is the most field passing through — loop square-on to the field, tilted, or edge-on?
Student. Square-on, surely. Tilt it and less passes; turn it edge-on and the lines slip past without entering — zero flux.
Socrates. Just so. We write it ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength times the area held perpendicular to it. And flux earns its own unit, the weber (Wb).
socratic.12:
Socrates. Now, since induction needs the flux to change, that little formula is really a list of what we may change. Look at B — how could you alter it?
Student. Strengthen or weaken the field, or move the loop nearer or further from the magnet.
Socrates. And A, the area within the field?
Student. Slide the loop in or out, so more or less of it sits in the field.
Socrates. And the angle?
Student. Rotate the loop — facing the field, then edge-on, then facing again. So three handles in all: field, area, and angle.
socratic.13:
Socrates. Faraday's law now puts a number on it: the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, times the number of turns — ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put it in words for me.
Student. More flux change in less time means a bigger emf — and a coil of N turns is driven N times over.
Socrates. So does it matter how large the flux is?
Student. No — only how fast it changes. The strongest field, held perfectly steady, induces nothing at all.
socratic.14:
Socrates. Of our three handles, which do you think the grid reaches for?
Student. Given that machine — a coil turning between magnets — it must be the third: the angle.
Socrates. And when a coil rotates in a steady field, what does its flux do?
Student. It swings — maximum, through zero, to maximum the other way, and back. The change never stops.
Socrates. That rotating coil carries the rest of our story — save for one exception we'll meet at the very end.
socratic.15:
Connects Socrates. And where is all this — flux, the weber, the three ways to change them — made exact?
Student. In Magnetic flux — the calculation for the perpendicular case, and qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
socratic.16:
Socrates. You'll have noticed Faraday's law carries a minus sign. It has a name of its own — Lenz's law, stated by Heinrich Lenz in 1834. It says the induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change that creates it. Push a magnet's north pole toward a coil — what does the coil do in return?
Student. If it opposes the change, the coil must present a north pole back, resisting the approach.
Socrates. And withdraw it?
Student. The current reverses and opposes the withdrawal instead. So the current opposes the change in flux — not the field itself.
socratic.17:
Socrates. Why must it oppose, though? Suppose it didn't — suppose the induced current aided the change. Follow that through.
Student. Then a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, pulling harder still — it would run away on its own.
Socrates. Producing what, from what?
Student. Energy out of a single small push — energy from nothing. Conservation forbids it.
Socrates. So Lenz's law is, at bottom, what?
Student. A consequence of energy conservation. Every joule induced must be paid for — as work done against the opposition.
socratic.18:
Socrates. Then tell me why a generator resists being turned.
Student. Connected to nothing, it spins freely. Connect a lamp and it grows harder to turn — that resistance is the energy the lamp draws.
Socrates. And a whole suburb?
Student. A power station's steam is needed just to keep the coil turning.
Socrates. Now reverse the picture — an electric car braking.
Student. Its motor runs as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force itself — storing the energy in the battery instead of throwing it away as heat in the pads.
socratic.19:
Connects Socrates. The direction rules — and the three-step recipe for getting them right?
Student. They live at Lenz's law — and the ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping from Position is stored energy.
socratic.20:
Socrates. Let us reconsider a machine you already met. Area of Study 2 gave you the DC motor — recall its parts.
Student. A coil on an axle between magnet poles. Feed current in, and the two sides of the coil are pushed opposite ways, so it rotates.
Socrates. And what keeps it turning the same way?
Student. A split-ring commutator, reversing the current every half-turn so the rotation never doubles back.
socratic.21:
Socrates. Now disconnect the battery and instead turn the coil by hand. Walk it through.
Student. Turning it changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out.
Socrates. So what has the motor become?
Student. A generator — the motor run backwards. A motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy.
Socrates. And how much of the diagram do we redraw?
Student. Not a single label — only the direction of energy flow is reversed.
socratic.22:
Socrates. One design choice is left: how does the spinning coil hand its current to the still, outside circuit? And that choice fixes the shape of the output. Try slip rings — two full rings, each tied to one end of the coil. What do they deliver?
Student. They stay in contact all the way round, so they give the emf just as it's made — reversing every half-turn. That's alternating current, AC. The machine would be an alternator.
Socrates. And the motor's own fitting, the split-ring commutator?
Student. It does the opposite — swapping the connections each half-turn, cancelling every reversal as it happens. So the outside current always flows one way: direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing.
Socrates. One spinning coil, then — and from one fitting or the other?
Student. Two entirely different outputs.
socratic.23:
Socrates. Take the crank yourself in Simulation 1.1 and turn it — watch which fitting gives which.
Student. I will.
socratic.24:
Connects Socrates. Where is each half of this mirror set out in full?
Student. The motor half at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
socratic.25:
Socrates. When the coil turns at a perfectly steady rate, what shape does the emf trace out?
Student. A sine wave — swinging positive, then negative, once per turn as the flux rises and falls.
Socrates. Australia's grid completes how many of those swings each second?
Student. Fifty — mains electricity at 50 Hz.
Socrates. We quote its size as 230 V. But the voltage at the wall actually swings between about +325 V and −325 V. How can both be true?
Student. Then the 230 V isn't a peak — it must be the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power.
Socrates. That equivalent average has a name: the root-mean-square, or rms, value. Pass 2 will explain where the name comes from.
socratic.26:
Socrates. Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a difficulty waits. Wire has resistance — and current through resistance does what?
Student. Turns electrical energy into heat. A loss.
Socrates. Given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Which quantity in there is squared?
Student. The current. So the current is what really punishes us.
socratic.27:
Socrates. Now, power is voltage times current. If I must deliver a fixed power, how might I send it?
Student. Either as low voltage with large current, or high voltage with small current.
Socrates. Raise the voltage tenfold — what becomes of the current, and of the heating loss?
Student. The current drops to a tenth, and since the loss goes as current squared, the heating falls to a hundredth.
Socrates. Over 170 km of line, then, is high voltage a luxury?
Student. No — a necessity.
socratic.28:
Socrates. What device lets us set the voltage where we please? It is Faraday's iron ring, grown up — the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves. Send alternating current into the primary — then what?
Student. It makes a changing flux; the core carries that flux through the secondary; and by Faraday's law an emf is induced there.
Socrates. Stepped up or down by what rule?
Student. In exact proportion to the turns on each coil — N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet.
Socrates. And when the voltage steps up?
Student. The current steps down by the same factor — the power, ideally, unchanged.
socratic.29:
Socrates. Here is a clue to a larger question. A transformer works only with changing flux. What would happen if we fed it steady DC?
Student. The flux would hold constant, nothing would be induced, and nothing would come out the other side.
Socrates. So why does the whole grid run on AC?
Student. Because without changing flux the transformers would be dead — and we couldn't step the voltage up and down at all.
socratic.30:
Socrates. So the journey in Figure 1.1 — what is it, really, repeated over and over?
Student. Faraday's law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces around 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the run to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a last transformer near the house brings it to 230 V at the wall — fifty times a second.
socratic.31:
Connects Socrates. And where is the arithmetic built properly — the rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss sums?
Student. In Transformers and transmission — and the full supply-system calculation, the exam's hardest regular question, waits in Transmission of electricity.
socratic.32:
Socrates. I promised one exception to the rotating coil. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells. What is curious about how they sit there?
Student. They don't move at all — they just lie still in the sun while current flows out of them.
Socrates. No coil, no magnet, no rotation. So what moves?
Student. Only electrons and light.
socratic.33:
Socrates. Look inside a cell. There is a thin layer of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light falls in — what does it do?
Student. It displaces electrons.
Socrates. And built permanently into the junction between the two slices is an electric field, which lets those displaced electrons cross one way only. What kind of current must that give?
Student. A one-way action makes a one-way current — so photovoltaic cells produce DC.
Socrates. And what does the work here — is a field still at the heart of it?
Student. Yes — an electric field this time, sitting at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
socratic.34:
Socrates. But your house and the grid run on AC, while the panels make DC. What must every rooftop system add?
Student. One more piece — an inverter, to turn the panels' DC into 230 V AC.
Socrates. And how deeply need we understand it?
Student. Only what it does, not how. The electronic circuitry — like the fine detail of the silicon — sits beyond the course.
socratic.35:
Socrates. One question lingers, though. Why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all?
Student. I can't answer that from anything we've said.
Socrates. No — it needs a new idea about the very nature of light: that it arrives in discrete packets of energy. That is the opening of Unit 4, where those packets get their name.
Student. Photons.
socratic.36:
Connects Socrates. Where are the cell and its inverter laid out, and where the reason light can eject electrons?
Student. The cell and inverter in Photovoltaic cells; the reason, in Unit 4's photoelectric effect.
socratic.37:
Socrates. We have now read Area of Study 3 once, in full. What does Pass 2 do with it?
Student. Retells it as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction — flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law and their graphs; Generators and alternators — the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics; and Transformers and transmission — rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic.
socratic.38:
Socrates. And Pass 3 — what is it for?
Student. Exam preparation: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors' advice and exam-style questions worked in full.
Socrates. Is it worth the labour?
Student. A steady 22 of the exam's 120 marks on every recent paper, with consistent recurring question types — and all of them appear there.
socratic.39:
Connects Socrates. So where do we go next, and where does the exam preparation live?
Student. Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction is the next chapter; and Pass 3 · Generation of electricity holds the exam preparation for this material.

curie.0: Area of Study 3 examines how fields produce mains electricity, and I trace the explanation down from a single deflection of a meter needle in 1831. The account runs from Faraday’s bench to the 500 kV lines leaving the Latrobe Valley; I solve nothing here, I only follow the thread.
curie.1: On a winter evening Melbourne draws its electrical load: kettles, trams, lighting and domestic switches. I can trace the wire behind a switch backwards through the wall, beneath the street, through a suburban substation to a terminal station on the city’s edge, then along the 500 kV lines running east across Gippsland. Approximately 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen, the wire terminates at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
curie.2: The machine is a coil of wire rotating in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam raised by burning brown coal provides the rotation; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Set aside the boilers and blades, and almost every large generator is the same device: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
curie.3: This Area of Study, titled How are fields used in electricity generation?, explains that device and the network built around it. I begin where the previous page concluded: with the observation that a field can be static or changing, and that the changing kind is the kind that produces electricity.
curie.4: Connects The previous page identified static versus changing as the consequential field classification. This Area of Study applies that idea to the grid; Unit 4 applies it again, to light itself.
curie.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday, formerly a bookbinder’s apprentice and originator of the field concept, had pursued one conviction for a decade. An electric current creates a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted had shown in 1820. It followed that a magnetic field should create a current. Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer, a sensitive current meter, connected to the second, expecting the steady current that symmetry appeared to predict.
curie.6: The needle registered nothing, or almost nothing: at the instant the battery was connected, it deflected, then returned to zero and stayed there, regardless of how strong and steady the current in the first coil was. At the instant of disconnection, it deflected again, in the opposite direction. The anticipated effect was absent; the observed effect occurred only in the two brief moments when the magnetic field was changing.
curie.7: By October the experiment had been reduced to its clearest form. Move a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle deflects. Hold the magnet stationary inside the coil, for any duration and with any strength of magnet, and no current appears. Withdraw it and the needle deflects in the opposite direction.
curie.8: Motion inward produces current in one direction; motion outward produces current in the other; no motion produces no current. The field does not drive charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
curie.9: The action a changing field exerts on charges is called an induced emf, short for electromotive force, a misleading historical name: it is not a force, and it is measured in volts. The phenomenon as a whole is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page reproduces Faraday’s effect deliberately and repeatedly. To say the field changes is not yet precise enough to base a grid on. The question is which quantity must change.
curie.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full set of rules for what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
curie.11: I picture the wire loop as a window frame and the magnetic field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s discovery depends is the magnetic flux: the amount of field passing through the loop. When the loop is square-on to the field the flux is greatest; when it is tilted less passes through; when it is edge-on the lines pass without entering, giving zero flux. The formula is named ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet, the field strength multiplied by the area held perpendicular to it, and flux has its own unit, the weber (Wb).
curie.12: Since induction requires changing flux, the formula lists the quantities that can be changed. B can be changed, by strengthening or weakening the field or by moving the loop nearer to or further from the magnet. A, the area within the field, can be changed by sliding the loop in or out. The angle can be changed, by rotating the loop so that it faces the field, then presents its edge, then faces it again: three quantities, namely field, area and angle.
curie.13: Faraday’s law states that the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put as a statement, more flux change in less time produces a larger emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. The magnitude of the flux does not matter; only its rate of change matters. The strongest field, held steady, induces nothing.
curie.14: Of the three quantities, the grid uses the third. When a coil rotates in a steady field its flux swings from maximum, through zero, to maximum in the other direction, and back, producing change that does not cease. This rotating coil is the basis of the remainder of the account, with one exception at the end.
curie.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
curie.16: Faraday’s law includes a minus sign, and that minus sign has its own name: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change creating it. Move a magnet’s north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so the coil presents a north pole in return, resisting the approach; withdraw the magnet and the current reverses, opposing the withdrawal. The wording is precise: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
curie.17: I can show why the opposition is necessary by considering the alternative. If the induced current aided the change, a displaced magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current and drawing it harder still, a runaway process producing energy without input, set off by a slight push. Energy conservation forbids this, and that is the content of Lenz’s law: it is a consequence of energy conservation. Every joule of electrical energy induced must be supplied as work done against the opposition.
curie.18: This is why a generator resists rotation. A generator connected to nothing rotates freely; connect a lamp and the rotation becomes harder, reflecting the energy the lamp consumes. Connect a suburb and a power station’s output of steam is required to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car reverses the arrangement: its motor acts as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, storing energy in the battery rather than dissipating it in the brake pads.
curie.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping introduced in Position is stored energy.
curie.20: I can now reconsider a machine studied earlier. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor: a coil on an axle between magnet poles; with current supplied, the two sides of the coil are pushed in opposite directions, producing rotation, while a split-ring commutator reverses the current every half-turn so that the rotation does not change direction.
curie.21: Disconnect the battery and rotate the coil, and the rotation changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The same hardware now functions as a generator, the reverse of the motor: a motor consumes electrical energy to produce motion; a generator consumes motion to produce electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays unchanged; only the direction of energy flow is reversed.
curie.22: One design choice remains, namely how the rotating coil transfers its current to the stationary external circuit, and it determines the form of the output. Slip rings, two complete rings each joined to one end of the coil, maintain continuous contact and deliver the emf as it is produced, reversing direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is called an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by exchanging the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it occurs, so that the external current always flows in one direction, namely direct current, DC, rising and falling but not reversing. One fitting yields two different outputs.
curie.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you operate the crank.
curie.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
curie.25: When the coil rotates at a perfectly steady rate the induced emf traces a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that the swing completes 50 cycles every second, mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its magnitude is quoted as 230 V, but that figure conceals a subtlety: the voltage at the wall actually swings between approximately +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains the name.
curie.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and the wall, however, a physics problem arises. Wire has resistance, and current flowing through resistance converts electrical energy into heat, a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. The quantity that is squared is the current.
curie.27: Power equals voltage multiplied by current, so the same power can be transmitted as a low voltage with a large current, or as a high voltage with a small current. Raise the voltage tenfold and the current falls to a tenth, the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is not a preference but a necessity.
curie.28: The device that makes voltage adjustable is a developed form of Faraday’s iron ring: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core; no part moves. Alternating current in the primary coil produces a changing flux; the core carries it through the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. When the voltage is stepped up the current steps down by the same factor, the power ideally unchanged.
curie.29: This also explains why the grid operates on AC: a transformer functions only with changing flux. With steady DC the flux stays constant; nothing is induced, and nothing is produced at the output.
curie.30: The journey shown in Figure 1.1 is therefore Faraday’s law applied repeatedly. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces approximately 20 kV; a step-up transformer raises it to 500 kV for transmission to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a final transformer near the house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second.
curie.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
curie.32: One generator type on the grid does not follow the rotating-coil template. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells, panels that stay motionless in the sun while current flows out of them. There is no coil, no magnet and no rotation; only electrons and light move.
curie.33: Inside each cell is a thin layer of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct slightly differently. Incident light displaces electrons, and an electric field, built permanently into the junction between the two slices, allows the displaced electrons to cross in one direction only. A one-way action produces a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field still performs the work, in this case the electric field, located at a junction rather than passing a coil.
curie.34: Houses and the grid operate on AC, so every rooftop system includes one further component: the inverter, which converts the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design requires only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic circuitry, like the detailed operation of the silicon, is beyond the course.
curie.35: One question remains: why light should be able to displace electrons from silicon at all. The answer requires a new idea about the nature of light, namely that it arrives in discrete packets of energy, and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where the packets are named photons.
curie.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
curie.37: Area of Study 3 has now been read once, in full. Pass 2 retells it as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each intended for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
curie.38: Pass 3 is for exam preparation: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked in full. The Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring question types are consistent; all of them appear there.
curie.39: Connects Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction is the next chapter; Pass 3 · Generation of electricity contains the exam preparation for this material.

mack.0: Area of Study 3 is the story of how fields end up powering your kettle — and the whole thing traces back to one twitch of a meter needle in 1831. We’ll follow the thread from Faraday’s workbench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley. Nothing gets solved here; we’re just laying out the route.
mack.1: Picture a winter evening in Melbourne, the whole city leaning on the grid at once: kettles, trams, lights, switches flicking on everywhere. Follow the wire behind any one of those switches and you can chase it backwards — through the wall, under the street, into a suburban substation, out to a terminal station at the city’s edge, then onto the 500 kV lines striding east across Gippsland. About 170 km from that Melbourne kitchen, the wire finally runs out at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
mack.2: The machine is a coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal does the spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, it’s falling water; out on the plains west of Melbourne, it’s moving air. mack.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the field distinction that really matters. This Area of Study turns that idea loose on the grid; Unit 4 turns it loose again, this time on light itself.

mack.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change it get pinned down properly in Magnetic flux — the calculation for the perpendicular case, and qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
mack.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that minus sign has earned its own name: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who spelled it out in 1834. The induced current always flows in whatever direction opposes the change that’s creating it. Push a magnet’s north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so the coil shoves a north pole right back, fighting the approach; pull the magnet away and the current flips, now fighting the retreat. The wording matters: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
mack.17: Why does it have to push back? Just imagine the alternative. If the induced current helped the change instead, a nudged magnet would get sucked in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a bigger current, hauling it in harder still — a runaway that spits out energy from nothing, all kicked off by one gentle shove. Energy conservation flatly forbids that, and that’s exactly what Lenz’s law is: energy conservation, wearing a different hat. Every joule of electrical energy you induce has to be paid for in work done against the opposition.
mack.18: This is why a generator fights back when you spin it. Hook a generator up to nothing and it turns freely; connect a lamp and suddenly the turning gets harder — that extra effort is the energy the lamp is eating. Connect a whole suburb and it takes a power station’s worth of steam to keep the coil moving. An electric car braking flips the whole arrangement around: its motor runs as a generator, the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, and the energy gets banked in the battery instead of being thrown away as heat in the brake pads.
mack.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for nailing them every time, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they’re protecting is the very same energy bookkeeping from Position is stored energy.
mack.20: There’s a machine from earlier we can now look at with fresh eyes. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor: a coil on an axle sitting between magnet poles. Feed in current and the two sides of the coil get pushed in opposite directions, so it spins, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn to keep the spin going the same way.
mack.21: Now pull out the battery and spin the coil by hand instead. The spinning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf pushes a current out. The very same hardware is now a generator — the motor running in reverse. A motor eats electrical energy to make motion; a generator eats motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays exactly where it was; all that flips is the direction the energy flows.
mack.22: One design choice is left, and it’s a big one: how does the spinning coil hand its current off to the stationary circuit outside? That choice decides the shape of the output. Slip rings — two complete rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep continuous contact and deliver the emf exactly as it’s made, flipping direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine gets called an alternator. The motor’s own gadget, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal the instant it happens, so the current outside always flows one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. Same coil, one fitting versus the other, two completely different outputs.
mack.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you turn the crank yourself.
mack.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror sits at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction gets examined almost every single year.
mack.25: When the coil spins at a perfectly steady rate, the induced emf traces out a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so the swing finishes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size gets quoted as 230 V, but that number hides a sneaky little subtlety: the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the very same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 unpacks where the name comes from.
mack.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, physics throws up a problem. Wire has resistance, and current shoving through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what gets squared: the current.
mack.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can ship the same power as low voltage with a big current, or high voltage with a tiny current. Bump the voltage up tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss, because it’s the current that’s squared, drops to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s the only way this works.
mack.28: The gadget that lets you dial voltage up or down is just a grown-up version of Faraday’s iron ring: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core ferries it through the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf over there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor, so the power — ideally — comes out unchanged.
mack.29: This is also why the grid runs on AC in the first place: a transformer only works with changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there constant; nothing gets induced, and nothing comes out the other end.
mack.30: So the journey in Figure 1.1 is really Faraday’s law applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer boosts it to 500 kV for the haul to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations walk it back down in stages; and a final transformer near the house hands you 230 V at the wall, 50 times a second.
mack.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic all get built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the hardest regular question on the exam — is waiting in Transmission of electricity.
mack.32: One kind of generator on the grid throws out the spinning-coil playbook entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now wear photovoltaic cells — panels that just sit dead still in the sun while current pours out of them. No coil, no magnet, no spinning; the only things moving are electrons and light.
mack.33: Inside each cell is a thin layer made of two slices of silicon, each doctored to conduct just a little differently. Light coming in knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction between the two slices — lets those loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way gate gives you a one-way current: photovoltaic cells make DC. A field is still doing the work here too — this time the electric field, parked at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
mack.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop setup carries one more piece: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design only asks you for what an inverter does, not how it pulls it off; the electronics inside it, like the fine print of the silicon, are beyond the course.
mack.35: One question still hangs in the air: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? Answering it takes a brand-new idea about what light actually is — that it shows up in discrete little packets of energy — and that’s the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets get a name: photons.
mack.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can boot electrons out at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
mack.37: That’s Area of Study 3, read through once, start to finish. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each one built for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the spinning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
mack.38: Pass 3 is for exam prep: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with all the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked through in full. This Area of Study has reliably been worth 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and the same question types keep coming back; you’ll find every one of them there.

levin.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields make the electricity that runs through your house, and the whole story unspools from a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. We follow it from Faraday’s crowded bench all the way to the 500 kV lines striding east out of the Latrobe Valley. Nothing here is solved. It is only traced.
levin.1: A winter evening, and Melbourne pulls hard on the wire — kettles, trams, lamps, the small click of a switch. Follow that wire backward: through the wall, under the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city’s edge, then out along the 500 kV lines that run east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from the kitchen where you flicked the switch, the wire ends at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
levin.2: And the machine is almost nothing — a coil of wire, turning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal does the turning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Strip away the boilers and the blades and nearly every great generator is the same plain thing: a coil of wire, spinning between magnets.
levin.3: This Area of Study, How are fields used in electricity generation?, is the story of that coil and the vast web humans built around it. It begins exactly where the last page left off — with the quiet observation that a field can sit still or shift, and that it is the shifting kind, only ever the shifting kind, that makes electricity.
levin.4: Connects The previous page named static versus changing as the one field distinction that matters. Here we hand that idea to the grid; in Unit 4 we hand it, again, to light itself.
levin.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, now the man who gave us the very idea of a field — had chased one conviction for ten years. A current makes a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown that in 1820. So surely, surely, a magnetic field must make a current in return. Faraday wound two coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a delicate meter for current — wired to the second, waiting for the steady answer that symmetry seemed to promise.
levin.6: The needle gave him nothing. Or almost nothing. At the instant he closed the battery it jumped, then sank back to zero and stayed there — no matter how strong, how steady, the current in the first coil ran. At the instant he broke the connection it jumped again, the other way. The effect he expected never came. The effect he got lived only in those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
levin.7: By October he had pared it down to its plainest shape. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire, and the needle swings. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as fierce a magnet as you like — and nothing flows. Pull it back out, and the needle swings the opposite way.
levin.8: In, and current runs one way; out, and it runs the other; still, and nothing runs at all. The field does not push charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
levin.9: What a changing field does to charges we call an induced emf — electromotive force, a misleading old name, because it is no force at all, and we measure it in volts. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is just Faraday’s twitch, performed on purpose, over and over, forever. But to say the field changes is too loose to build a grid on. The real question is sharper: which quantity must change?
levin.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full set of rules for what the meter sees and what it ignores, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
levin.11: Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s whole discovery turns is the magnetic flux — how much field threads the loop. Square-on to the field, the flux is greatest; tilt the loop, and less slips through; turn it edge-on, and the lines slide past without entering at all, and the flux falls to zero. We name it ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — field strength times the area you hold across it — and flux gets a unit all its own, the weber (Wb).
levin.12: Since induction needs changing flux, the formula is really a list of the things you can change. B, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by carrying the loop toward the magnet or away. A, the area caught in the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And the angle, by rotating the loop so it faces the field, then turns its edge, then faces it again. Three handles only: field, area, angle.
levin.13: Faraday’s law says the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: more flux change in less time gives a bigger emf, and a coil of N turns feels it N times over. How much flux there is does not matter at all. Only how fast it changes. The strongest field in the world, held perfectly still, induces nothing.
levin.14: Of the three handles, the grid reaches for the third. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from a maximum, down through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back — a change that never stops. This turning coil carries the rest of the story, save for one exception waiting at the end.
levin.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made exact in Magnetic flux — the calculation for the square-on case, the reasoning for every tilt in between.
levin.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that minus sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who set it down in 1834. The induced current always flows so as to oppose the very change that made it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current circles so the coil turns its own north pole back, pushing against the approach; withdraw the magnet, and the current reverses to resist the leaving. The wording is exact: the current opposes the change in flux, never the field itself.
levin.17: Why must it oppose? Imagine if it did not. Suppose the induced current helped the change along — then a nudged magnet would be pulled in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, pulling harder still: a runaway, energy pouring out of a single gentle push, from nothing. The conservation of energy forbids it. That, and only that, is the content of Lenz’s law — it is energy conservation wearing a different coat. Every joule of electrical energy you induce must be paid for in work done against the opposition.
levin.18: This is why a generator fights you. Connected to nothing, it turns freely; hook up a lamp and the turning grows heavier, the weight of exactly the energy the lamp burns. Hook up a suburb and it takes a power station’s steam just to keep the coil moving. An electric car braking flips the whole arrangement: its motor becomes a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, pouring the energy back into the battery instead of throwing it away in the brake pads.
levin.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping opened in Position is stored energy.
levin.20: A machine you have already met can now be read backward. Area of Study 2 gave you the DC motor — a coil on an axle between magnet poles; feed it current, and the two sides of the coil are pushed in opposite directions, and it turns, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn so the spin never doubles back.
levin.21: Now disconnect the battery and turn the coil by hand. The turning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The same hardware is now a generator — the motor run in reverse. A motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays exactly where it was; only the direction of the energy reverses.
levin.22: One design choice is left — how the spinning coil hands its current to the still circuit outside — and it decides the shape of the output. Slip rings, two whole rings each tied to one end of the coil, keep unbroken contact and pass the emf out just as it is made, flipping direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the very opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each flip just as it arrives, so the outside current always runs one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. One fitting, two utterly different outputs.
levin.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you work the crank.
levin.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every single year.
levin.25: Turn the coil at a perfectly even rate and the induced emf traces a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once each turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing completes 50 cycles a second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. We quote its size as 230 V, but that figure hides something: the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains where the name comes from.
levin.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and the wall, though, physics raises an objection. Wire has resistance, and current pushing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what gets squared. The current.
levin.27: Power is voltage times current, so the same power can travel as a low voltage with a large current, or a high voltage with a small one. Lift the voltage tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is not a preference. It is a necessity.
levin.28: The device that lets you tune the voltage is Faraday’s iron ring, grown up: the transformer. Two coils share a single iron core; nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary makes a changing flux; the core carries it through the secondary; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor — the power, ideally, untouched.
levin.29: And this is why the grid runs on AC: a transformer breathes only on changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux holds constant; nothing is induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
levin.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is just Faraday’s law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the run to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations bring it back down in stages; and a last transformer near the house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times every second.
levin.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
levin.32: One kind of generator on the grid breaks the rotating-coil pattern entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that lie perfectly still in the sun while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no spinning. Only the electrons and the light are moving.
levin.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich: two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light falling in knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction between the two slices — lets those loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way gate makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells give DC. A field still does the work; here it is the electric field, standing at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
levin.34: But houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more piece: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronics, like the fine workings of the silicon, lie beyond the course.
levin.35: One question lingers: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? The answer needs a wholly new idea about what light is — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given a name: photons.
levin.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.

czerski.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf draws out a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once each turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size gets quoted as 230 V, but that figure hides something sneaky: the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would hand you the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains where the name comes from.
czerski.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a physics problem rears up. Wire has resistance, and current pushing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what gets squared in there: the current.
czerski.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can ship the same power as a low voltage with a big current, or as a high voltage with a small current. Crank the voltage up tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss, because the current is squared, drops to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s a must.
czerski.28: The device that lets you dial the voltage up and down is a grown-up version of Faraday’s iron ring: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core ferries it across to the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the very same factor — the power, ideally, untouched.
czerski.29: This is also why the grid runs on AC in the first place: a transformer only works with changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there constant; nothing gets induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
czerski.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is really just Faraday’s law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer hauls it to 500 kV for the haul to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a last transformer near the house drops it to 230 V at the wall, 50 times a second.
czerski.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
czerski.32: There’s one generator on the grid that breaks the spinning-coil mould entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now wear photovoltaic cells — panels that sit dead still in the sun while current pours out of them. No coil, no magnet, no spinning anything; the only things moving are electrons and light.
czerski.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich: two slices of silicon, each doctored to conduct just a little differently. Light landing on it knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction where the two slices meet — lets those freed electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way gate gives you a one-way current: photovoltaic cells make DC. A field is still doing the work, only here it’s the electric field, parked at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
czerski.34: Houses and the grid both run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more part: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design only asks what an inverter does, not how it pulls it off; its electronics, like the fine detail of the silicon, sit beyond the course.
czerski.35: One question is still hanging: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? Answering it needs a brand-new idea about what light actually is — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and that’s the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets get a name: photons.
czerski.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
czerski.37: Area of Study 3 has now had its first read-through, end to end. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the spinning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
czerski.38: Pass 3 is for exam prep: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked through in full. This Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and the question types keep coming back the same; you’ll find all of them there.
