feynman.0: Area of Study 3 is about how fields make the mains electricity that runs your house — and the whole story climbs out of one tiny twitch of a meter needle back in 1831. We’ll follow it from Faraday’s workbench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley. Nothing gets solved here; we’re just tracing the thread.
feynman.1: Picture a winter evening in Melbourne: kettles boiling, trams running, lights on, switches flicking. Take the wire behind one of those switches and follow it backwards — through the wall, under the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the edge of the city, then out along the 500 kV lines heading east across Gippsland. About 170 km from that Melbourne kitchen, the wire finally arrives at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
feynman.2: And the machine is just a coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal does the spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; out on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Strip away the boilers and the blades and nearly every big generator is the same gadget: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
feynman.3: This Area of Study, called How are fields used in electricity generation?, is about that gadget and the network we’ve built around it. It picks up exactly where the last page left off — with the idea that a field can sit still or change, and that it’s the changing kind that makes electricity.
feynman.4: Connects The previous page picked out static versus changing as the field distinction that really matters. This Area of Study takes that idea and points it at the grid; Unit 4 takes it again and points it at light itself.
feynman.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, and the man who dreamed up the field idea in the first place — had been chasing one hunch for ten years. An electric current makes a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown that in 1820. So surely, Faraday reasoned, a magnetic field ought to make a current. He wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, pushed a steady current through the first one, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — hooked to the second, fully expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
feynman.6: The needle did nothing — or just about nothing. The instant he connected the battery it gave a little kick, then dropped back to zero and stayed there, no matter how strong and steady he made the current in the first coil. Then, at the instant he disconnected it, the needle kicked again — the other way. The effect he’d been hoping for simply wasn’t there; the effect he actually got showed up only in those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
feynman.7: By October he’d boiled the experiment down to its cleanest form. Shove a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle kicks. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as strong a magnet as you like — and nothing happens. Pull it back out and the needle kicks the other way.
feynman.8: In gives a current one way; out gives a current the other way; sitting still gives nothing at all. The field doesn’t push the charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
feynman.9: What a changing field does to charges we call an induced emf — short for electromotive force, which is a lousy name handed down by history: it isn’t a force, and we measure it in volts. The whole business is called electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is just Faraday’s little effect, done on purpose, over and over. But saying “the field changes” isn’t sharp enough to build a grid on. The real question is: which quantity has to change?
feynman.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with the full set of rules for what shows up on the meter and what doesn’t, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
feynman.11: Think of the wire loop as a window frame and the magnetic field lines as threads poking through the opening. The thing Faraday’s discovery hangs on is the magnetic flux: how much field gets through the loop. Hold the loop square-on to the field and the flux is biggest; tilt it and less gets through; turn it edge-on and the lines slide past without entering at all — zero flux. We write it ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet, the field strength times the area held square-on to it, and flux gets its own unit, the weber (Wb).
feynman.12: Since induction needs changing flux, the formula is really a menu of things you’re allowed to change. You can change B — make the field stronger or weaker, or move the loop closer to or further from the magnet. You can change A, the area inside the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And you can change the angle, by twisting the loop so it faces the field, then turns its edge to it, then faces it again. Three knobs to turn: field, area and angle.
feynman.13: Faraday’s law says the induced emf equals how fast the flux is changing, times the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. In plain words: more flux change in less time gives you a bigger emf, and a coil of N turns gets driven N times over. How much flux there is doesn’t matter a bit — only how fast it’s changing. The strongest field in the world, held dead still, induces nothing.
feynman.14: Of those three knobs, the grid grabs the third. When a coil spins in a steady field its flux swings from a maximum, down through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back again — a change that never quits. This spinning coil is what the rest of the story is built on, with one exception waiting at the end.
feynman.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change it get pinned down properly in Magnetic flux — the calculation for the square-on case, and the reasoning for every angle in between.
feynman.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that minus sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who spelled it out in 1834. The induced current flows in whatever direction fights the change that made it. Push a magnet’s north pole toward a coil and the induced current circles around so the coil shows a north pole right back, pushing against the approach; pull the magnet away and the current flips, now tugging against the retreat. The wording matters: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
feynman.17: You can see why the opposition has to be there just by imagining the opposite. Suppose the induced current helped the change instead. Then a nudged magnet would get sucked in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a bigger current, pulling it in harder still — a runaway machine cranking out energy from nothing, all from one little shove. Energy conservation flatly forbids that, and that’s exactly what Lenz’s law is: a consequence of energy conservation. Every joule of electrical energy you induce, you have to pay for as work done against the opposition.
feynman.18: That’s why a generator fights back when you try to turn it. Connect a generator to nothing and it spins freely; hook up a lamp and suddenly it’s harder to turn — that extra effort is exactly the energy the lamp is eating. Connect a whole suburb and you need a power station’s worth of steam just to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car runs the whole thing backwards: its motor acts as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, stuffing energy into the battery instead of wasting it as heat in the brake pads.
feynman.19: Connects The direction rules, plus the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. And the ledger they’re protecting is the same energy bookkeeping we met in Position is stored energy.
feynman.20: Now we can take a second look at a machine we’ve already met. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor: a coil on an axle sitting between the poles of a magnet. Feed in current and the two sides of the coil get pushed in opposite directions, so it spins, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn so the spin keeps going the same way.
feynman.21: Now disconnect the battery and spin the coil by hand instead. The spinning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The very same hardware is now a generator — the motor run in reverse. A motor eats electrical energy to make motion; a generator eats motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays exactly the same; only the direction the energy flows gets flipped.
feynman.22: One design choice is left: how the spinning coil hands its current off to the stationary outside circuit — and that choice decides the shape of the output. Slip rings — two complete rings, each tied to one end of the coil — keep unbroken contact and pass the emf along just as it’s made, flipping direction every half-turn. That’s alternating current, AC, and the machine is called an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite job: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each flip as it happens, so the outside current always runs one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. Same coil, two different fittings, two different outputs.

feynman.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror sits at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings difference gets asked just about every single year.
feynman.25: When the coil spins at a perfectly steady rate the induced emf traces out a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once every turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. We quote its size as 230 V, but that number hides a subtlety: the voltage at the wall actually swings between about +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains where the name comes from.
feynman.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a physics problem shows up. Wire has resistance, and current pushing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice what’s getting squared: the current.
feynman.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can send the same power as a low voltage with a big current, or as a high voltage with a small current. Crank the voltage up tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss drops to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage isn’t a nice-to-have, it’s a must.
feynman.28: The gadget that lets you dial the voltage up or down is a grown-up version of Faraday’s iron ring: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries it through the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor, so the power, ideally, stays put.
feynman.29: This also tells you why the grid runs on AC: a transformer only works with changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there constant; nothing gets induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
feynman.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is really Faraday’s law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer hoists it to 500 kV for the trip to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and one last transformer near the house hands you 230 V at the wall, 50 times a second.
feynman.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic all get built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the hardest question the exam reliably throws at you — waits in Transmission of electricity.
feynman.32: One kind of generator on the grid doesn’t play by the spinning-coil rules at all. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that just sit dead still in the sun while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no spinning; only electrons and light are moving.
feynman.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light coming in knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction between the two slices — lets those loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way gate gives a one-way current: photovoltaic cells make DC. A field is still doing the work; this time it’s the electric field, parked at a junction instead of sweeping past a coil.
feynman.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more piece: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design only asks you what an inverter does, not how it pulls it off; its electronic guts, like the fine print of the silicon, are beyond the course.

Answering it needs a brand-new idea about what light actually is — that it shows up in discrete packets of energy — and that’s the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets get a name: photons.
feynman.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can knock electrons out at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
feynman.37: That’s Area of Study 3, read through once, the whole way. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the spinning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).

sagan.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields summon the electricity that runs through our walls, and the whole astonishing answer descends from a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. The story reaches from Faraday’s cluttered bench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley — and here we solve nothing; we only trace the thread.
sagan.1: On a winter evening, Melbourne reaches out and draws its electrical load: kettles, trams, lamps, the small ordinary click of a switch. Follow the wire behind that switch backwards — through the wall, beneath the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city’s edge, and out along the 500 kV lines striding east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen, the wire ends at last in a single machine in the Latrobe Valley.
sagan.2: And the machine is nothing more than a coil of wire turning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley the turning comes from steam raised by burning brown coal; in the Snowy Mountains, from falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, from the moving air itself. Strip away the boilers and the blades, and almost every great generator on Earth is the same humble device: a coil of wire, spinning between magnets.
sagan.3: This Area of Study, titled How are fields used in electricity generation?, is the tale of that device and the vast network we have built around it. It begins exactly where the previous page left off: with the realisation that a field can sit still or change, and that it is the changing kind — only the changing kind — that gives us electricity.
sagan.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the distinction that would matter most. This Area of Study carries that single idea into the grid; Unit 4 will carry it further still, all the way to light itself.
sagan.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, now the author of the very idea of a field — had chased one conviction for ten long years. An electric current makes a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted had revealed in 1820. Surely, then, a magnetic field ought to make a current. So Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, sent a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive meter for current — wired to the second, waiting for the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
sagan.6: The needle gave him nothing, or very nearly nothing: at the instant he connected the battery it leapt, then fell back to zero and stayed there, no matter how strong and unwavering the current in the first coil became. At the instant he broke the connection it leapt again — the other way. The effect he had expected never came; the effect he found appeared only in those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
sagan.7: By October he had pared the experiment down to its purest form. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle jumps. Hold the magnet perfectly still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as fierce a magnet as you please — and nothing flows at all. Draw it out again, and the needle jumps the opposite way.
sagan.8: Motion inward gives current one way; motion outward gives it the other; stillness gives nothing. It is not the field that drives charges through the wire. It is the change in the field.
sagan.9: The push a changing field gives to charges we call an induced emf — short for electromotive force, a misleading old name, for it is no force at all and we measure it in volts. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is Faraday’s effect, conjured up deliberately and tirelessly, over and over. But to say simply that “the field changes” is far too loose a thing on which to hang a grid. The real question is: which quantity must change?
sagan.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full catalogue of what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
sagan.11: Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as so many threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s whole discovery turns is the magnetic flux: how much field threads its way through the loop. Hold the loop square-on to the field and the flux is greatest; tilt it and less gets through; turn it edge-on and the lines slip past without entering at all, leaving zero flux. The formula bears the name ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength times the area we hold perpendicular to it — and flux has a unit all its own, the weber (Wb).
sagan.12: Since induction demands changing flux, the formula itself lays out everything we are allowed to change. B we can change, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by carrying the loop nearer to or further from the magnet. A, the area lying within the field, we can change by sliding the loop in or out. And the angle we can change, by turning the loop so it faces the field, then shows it its edge, then faces it once more: three quantities in all — field, area and angle.
sagan.13: Faraday’s law tells us the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: more flux change in less time gives a larger emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. How large the flux happens to be matters not at all; only how fast it changes. The mightiest field, held perfectly steady, induces nothing whatever.
sagan.14: Of the three quantities, the grid reaches for the third. Let a coil turn in a steady field, and its flux swings from a maximum, down through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back again — a change that never stops. This turning coil is the foundation for the whole of the rest of the story, with a single exception saved for the end.
sagan.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made exact in Magnetic flux — the calculation for the perpendicular case, and qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
sagan.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that minus sign has earned a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who set it down in 1834. The induced current always flows in the direction that opposes the very change that made it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so the coil answers with a north pole of its own, fighting the approach; pull the magnet away and the current reverses, now resisting the retreat. The words are chosen with care: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
sagan.17: Why must there be opposition? Imagine for a moment the opposite. If the induced current helped the change along, a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, the flux would change faster, a larger current would arise, and it would haul the magnet harder still — a runaway, pouring out energy from a single gentle push and nothing more. The conservation of energy forbids it, and that, in truth, is all Lenz’s law is: a consequence of energy conservation. Every joule of electrical energy we induce must first be paid for, as work done against the opposition.
sagan.18: This is why a generator fights the hand that turns it. Connected to nothing, a generator spins freely; connect a single lamp and the turning grows harder, by exactly the energy the lamp drinks up. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the very opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it happens, so the outside current always runs one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never turning back. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic circuitry, like the fine workings of the silicon, lies beyond the course.

The answer demands a wholly new idea about the very nature of light — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given their name: photons.

tyson.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields make the mains electricity in your wall — and the whole story unspools from one twitch of a meter needle back in 1831. We’ll trace it from Faraday’s bench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley. Nothing gets solved here. We’re just following the wire.
tyson.1: Picture a winter evening in Melbourne, the whole city pulling its electrical load — kettles, trams, lights, switches flicking on. Follow the wire behind one switch: back through the wall, under the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city’s edge, then out along the 500 kV lines striking east across Gippsland. Roughly 170 km from that Melbourne kitchen, the wire finally stops — at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
tyson.2: And what is that machine? A coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. That’s it. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal does the spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; out on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Strip away the boilers and the blades and almost every big generator on Earth is the same trick: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
tyson.3: This Area of Study, How are fields used in electricity generation?, is the story of that machine and the network we built around it. And it picks up exactly where the last page left off — with the realisation that a field can sit still or change, and it’s the changing kind that makes electricity.
tyson.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the field distinction that matters. This Area of Study aims that idea at the grid; Unit 4 aims it again, at light itself.
tyson.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, now the man who dreamed up the field — had been chasing one hunch for ten years. An electric current makes a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown that back in 1820. So flip it: a magnetic field ought to make a current. Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — wired to the second, fully expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
tyson.6: The needle did nothing — or almost nothing. The instant he connected the battery it kicked, snapped back to zero, and sat there, no matter how strong and steady the current in the first coil got. The instant he disconnected, it kicked again — the other way. The effect he’d predicted never showed. The effect he actually got happened only in those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
tyson.7: By October he’d boiled the experiment down to its purest form. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire — the needle jumps. Hold the magnet dead still inside the coil, for as long as you like, with any strength of magnet — nothing. Pull it back out — the needle jumps the opposite way.
tyson.8: In one direction the current goes one way; out the other; no motion, no current. So the field doesn’t drive charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
tyson.9: What a changing field does to charges goes by the name induced emf — short for electromotive force, which is honestly a terrible name: it isn’t a force, and it’s measured in volts. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is just Faraday’s effect, reproduced on purpose, over and over. But “the field changes” is way too vague to run a grid on. The real question is: which quantity has to change?
tyson.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with the full set of rules for what the meter registers and what it ignores, kicks off Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
tyson.11: Think of the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity Faraday’s discovery hangs on is the magnetic flux — how much field actually gets through the loop. Square-on to the field, the flux is at its biggest; tilt the loop and less gets through; turn it edge-on and the lines slide right past without entering, so the flux is zero. The formula has a name — ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength times the area held perpendicular to it, and flux gets its own unit, the weber (Wb).
tyson.12: Since induction needs changing flux, the formula is basically a shopping list of things you can change. You can change B — crank the field up or down, or move the loop nearer or further from the magnet. You can change A, the area inside the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And you can change the angle, by rotating the loop so it faces the field, then turns its edge, then faces it again. Three knobs to turn: field, area and angle.
tyson.13: Faraday’s law says the induced emf equals the rate the flux changes, times the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. In plain words: more flux change in less time means a bigger emf, and a coil of N turns gets driven N times over. How big the flux is? Doesn’t matter at all. Only how fast it changes. The strongest field in the world, held steady, induces precisely nothing.
tyson.14: Of the three knobs, the grid reaches for the third. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from maximum, down through zero, to maximum the other way, and back — change that never stops. That spinning coil is the backbone of everything that follows, with one exception saved for the end.
tyson.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change it get pinned down in Magnetic flux — the calculation for the perpendicular case, and the qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
tyson.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that minus sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current flows in whatever direction opposes the change that created it. Push a magnet’s north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so the coil shoves a north pole right back, fighting the approach; pull the magnet away and the current flips, now fighting the retreat. Read the wording carefully: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
tyson.17: Why must it oppose? Picture the alternative. Say the induced current helped the change: a nudged magnet would get sucked in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a bigger current, pulling it in harder still — a runaway loop spitting out energy from nothing, all from one little shove. Energy conservation says no chance. And that’s really all Lenz’s law is — energy conservation in disguise. Every joule of electrical energy you induce has to be paid for in work done against the opposition.
tyson.18: This is exactly why a generator fights back when you turn it. Hook a generator up to nothing and it spins freely; connect a lamp and suddenly it’s harder to turn — that resistance is the energy the lamp is eating. Connect a whole suburb and it takes a power station’s worth of steam to keep the coil spinning. A braking electric car runs the trick backwards: its motor becomes a generator, the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, and the energy goes into the battery instead of getting wasted as heat in the brake pads.
tyson.19: Connects The direction rules, plus the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. And the ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping introduced in Position is stored energy.
tyson.20: Here’s a machine you’ve already met, seen with fresh eyes. Area of Study 2 gave you the DC motor: a coil on an axle between magnet poles; feed in current and the two sides of the coil get pushed opposite ways, so it spins, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn to keep the spin going one way.
tyson.21: Now yank out the battery and spin the coil by hand. The spinning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The exact same hardware is now a generator — the motor running in reverse. A motor eats electrical energy to make motion; a generator eats motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays put. All that flips is the direction the energy flows.
tyson.22: One design choice is left — how the spinning coil hands its current off to the stationary outside circuit — and that one choice decides the shape of the output. Slip rings — two complete rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep continuous contact and deliver the emf exactly as it’s made, reversing direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and we call the machine an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal right as it happens, so the outside current always flows one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. One fitting, two completely different outputs.
tyson.23: In Simulation 1.1 you get to turn the crank yourself.
tyson.24: Connects The motor half of this mirror sits at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and that commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction gets examined almost every single year.
tyson.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf traces a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. We quote its size as 230 V, but that number hides a twist: the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value — Pass 2 unpacks where the name comes from.
tyson.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, physics throws up a problem. Wire has resistance, and current pushing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. And notice what gets squared: the current.
tyson.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can ship the same power as low voltage with a big current, or high voltage with a small current. Crank the voltage up tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage isn’t a nice-to-have. It’s the whole ballgame.
tyson.28: The gadget that makes voltage adjustable is just Faraday’s iron ring, all grown up: the transformer. Two coils sharing an iron core, and nothing moves at all. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries it across to the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there — stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor — the power, ideally, untouched.
tyson.29: This is also why the grid runs on AC in the first place: a transformer only works with changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there — nothing gets induced, and nothing comes out the other end.
tyson.30: So the journey in Figure 1.1 is really Faraday’s law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces roughly 20 kV; a step-up transformer hauls it to 500 kV for the trip to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and a final transformer near your house drops it to 230 V at the wall — 50 times a second.
tyson.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic all get built properly in Transformers and transmission; and the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s single hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
tyson.32: One generator on the grid breaks the spinning-coil mould entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now wear photovoltaic cells — panels that sit dead still in the sun while current pours out of them. No coil, no magnet, no rotation. The only things moving are electrons and light.
tyson.33: Inside each cell sits a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light comes in and knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction between the two slices — only lets those loose electrons cross one way. A one-way action makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field is still doing the work — this time the electric field, parked at a junction instead of sweeping past a coil.
tyson.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more component: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design only asks what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic guts, like the fine detail of the silicon, sit beyond this course.
tyson.35: Which leaves one nagging question: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? Answering it demands a brand-new idea about what light actually is — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and that’s the opening act of Unit 4, where those packets get a name: photons.
tyson.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; and the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
tyson.37: That’s Area of Study 3, read once, start to finish. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each one built for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
tyson.38: Pass 3 is built for exam prep: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked all the way through. This Area of Study has pulled a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring question types barely change; every one of them shows up there.

greene.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday—once a bookbinder’s apprentice, now the man who gave us the very idea of a field—had chased a single conviction for a decade. An electric current makes a magnetic field; Hans Christian Oersted had shown as much in 1820. Surely, then, symmetry demanded the reverse: a magnetic field ought to make a current. So Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer—a sensitive current meter—wired to the second, fully expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
greene.6: The needle did nothing—or very nearly nothing. At the instant he connected the battery it flicked, then fell back to zero and stayed there, no matter how strong and how steady the current in the first coil became. At the instant he disconnected it, the needle flicked again, this time the other way. The effect he had predicted simply was not there; the effect he actually saw appeared only in the two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
greene.7: By October he had pared the experiment down to its purest form. Thrust a bar magnet into a coil of wire, and the needle jumps. Hold the magnet dead still inside the coil—for as long as you like, with as fierce a magnet as you like—and nothing flows. Pull it back out, and the needle jumps the opposite way.
greene.8: Motion inward gives a current one way; motion outward gives it the other way; no motion gives nothing at all. The field itself does not push charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
greene.9: We call this action that a changing field works on charges an induced emf—short for electromotive force, a name that misleads on two counts at once: it is not a force, and we measure it in volts. The phenomenon as a whole is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is simply Faraday’s effect, staged deliberately and repeated endlessly. But “the field changes” is far too vague to hang a grid on. The real question is sharper: which quantity must change?
greene.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full rulebook for what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
greene.11: Think of the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads stitched through the opening. The quantity Faraday’s discovery truly hinges on is the magnetic flux: how much field passes through the loop. Hold the loop square-on to the field and the flux is greatest; tilt it and less slips through; turn it edge-on and the lines glide past without entering at all, leaving zero flux. The formula bears its own name, ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet—the field strength times the area held perpendicular to it—and flux even earns its own unit, the weber (Wb).
greene.12: Because induction lives on changing flux, this little formula is really a menu of things you can change. You can change B, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by sliding the loop nearer to or further from the magnet. You can change A, the area sitting in the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And you can change the angle, by rotating the loop so it faces the field, then turns its edge, then faces it again. Three handles in all: field, area and angle.
greene.13: Faraday’s law says the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: the more flux change you cram into the less time, the larger the emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. The sheer size of the flux is beside the point; only how fast it changes matters. The strongest field in the world, held perfectly still, induces precisely nothing.
greene.14: Of the three handles, the grid reaches for the third. Spin a coil at a steady rate in a steady field, and its flux swings from a maximum, down through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back again—a change that never stops. This spinning coil is the heart of everything that follows, with a single exception waiting at the end.
greene.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
greene.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that humble minus sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who put it into words in 1834. The induced current always flows in the direction that opposes the very change that created it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current circulates so the coil answers with a north pole of its own, pushing back against the approach; pull the magnet away, and the current reverses, now resisting the retreat. The wording must be exact: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
greene.17: Why must it oppose? Imagine for a moment the alternative. Suppose the induced current helped the change. Then a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, hauling it in harder still—a runaway that manufactures energy out of a single gentle push. Energy conservation forbids it flat out, and that is the whole content of Lenz’s law: it is energy conservation wearing a different hat. Every joule of electrical energy you induce must first be paid for as work done against the opposition.
greene.18: This is exactly why a generator fights back when you turn it. Connect a generator to nothing and it spins freely; hang a lamp on it and the spinning grows harder, mirroring the energy the lamp draws. Wire up a whole suburb and it takes a power station’s worth of steam just to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car runs the whole story in reverse: its motor becomes a generator, the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, and the energy pours into the battery instead of vanishing as heat in the brake pads.
greene.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping introduced in Position is stored energy.
greene.20: Now we can revisit a machine we have already met. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor: a coil on an axle slung between magnet poles; feed it current, the two sides of the coil are shoved in opposite directions, and the coil rotates—while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn so the rotation never doubles back on itself.
greene.21: Disconnect the battery and turn the coil by hand instead, and watch what happens. The rotation changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The very same hardware is now a generator—the motor run backwards: a motor spends electrical energy to make motion, a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays exactly where it was; only the direction of energy flow has been turned around.
greene.22: One design choice still remains—how the spinning coil hands its current to the stationary outside circuit—and it alone decides the shape of the output. Slip rings, two complete rings each tied to one end of the coil, keep continuous contact and deliver the emf just as the coil makes it, flipping direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine earns the name alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the very opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each flip the moment it arrives, so the external current always heads one way—direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. One fitting, two utterly different outputs.
greene.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you work the crank yourself.
greene.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
greene.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf traces out a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing completes 50 cycles every second—mains electricity at 50 Hz. We quote its size as 230 V, but that single number hides a subtlety: the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains where the name comes from.
greene.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a piece of physics gets in the way. Wire has resistance, and a current forced through resistance bleeds electrical energy away as heat—a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice carefully which quantity is squared: it is the current.
greene.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can ship the same power as a low voltage with a fat current, or as a high voltage with a thin one. Raise the voltage tenfold and the current drops to a tenth—and the heating loss, squared as it is, to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage stops being a preference and becomes a necessity.
greene.28: The device that makes voltage adjustable is Faraday’s iron ring, all grown up: the transformer. Two coils share a single iron core, and not one part of it moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core ferries that flux through the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or stepped down in exact proportion to the turns wound on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up, and the current steps down by the very same factor—the power, ideally, untouched.
greene.29: And here is why the grid runs on AC in the first place: a transformer works only with changing flux. Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there, unchanging; nothing is induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
greene.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is nothing but Faraday’s law, applied over and over. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces roughly 20 kV; a step-up transformer hoists it to 500 kV for the run to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations walk it back down in stages; and a final transformer near your house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second.
greene.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
greene.32: One kind of generator on the grid refuses to follow the spinning-coil template. More than four million Australian rooftops now wear photovoltaic cells—panels that sit perfectly still in the sun while current streams out of them. No coil, no magnet, no rotation; only electrons and light are on the move.
greene.33: Inside each cell lies a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct just a little differently. Incoming light knocks electrons loose, and an electric field—built permanently into the junction where the two slices meet—lets those loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way action begets a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field is still doing the work, only now it is the electric field, standing guard at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
greene.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system adds one more piece: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic circuitry, like the fine detail inside the silicon, lies beyond the course.
greene.35: Just one question lingers: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? The answer demands a wholly new idea about the nature of light—that it arrives in discrete packets of energy—and it is the very first topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given a name: photons.
greene.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
greene.37: Area of Study 3 has now been read once, all the way through. Pass 2 retells it as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each pitched for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the spinning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
greene.38: Pass 3 is built for the exam itself: two chapters laid out in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions all worked through in full. This Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring question types hardly waver; you will meet every one of them there.

If the induced current helped the change, a nudged magnet would get yanked in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a bigger current, yanking it harder still — a runaway loop cranking out energy from nothing, all from one little push! Energy conservation flat-out forbids that, and that’s exactly what Lenz’s law is: a consequence of energy conservation. Every joule of electrical energy you induce has to be paid for as work done against the opposition.
nye.18: And that’s why a generator fights back when you turn it! Hook a generator up to nothing and it spins free as you like; connect a lamp and suddenly it’s harder to turn — that extra effort is the energy the lamp is gobbling up. Connect a whole suburb, and now you need a power station full of steam just to keep the coil spinning. A braking electric car flips the whole thing around: its motor runs as a generator, the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, and the energy goes back into the battery instead of getting burned off in the brake pads!
nye.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. And the energy ledger they’re protecting is the very same bookkeeping we set up in Position is stored energy.
nye.20: Now let’s revisit a machine we’ve already met! Area of Study 2 gave us the DC motor: a coil on an axle sitting between magnet poles. Feed in a current and the two sides of the coil get pushed in opposite directions, so it spins — while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn so the spin never reverses.
nye.21: But check this out — disconnect the battery and spin the coil yourself! The spinning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. Same exact hardware, now working as a generator — the motor run backwards! A motor eats electrical energy to make motion; a generator eats motion to make electrical energy. Every single label on the motor diagram stays put — only the direction the energy flows gets flipped.
nye.22: There’s one design choice left, and it’s a big one: how does the spinning coil hand its current off to the still external circuit? Because that choice decides what the output looks like! Slip rings — two complete rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep continuous contact and deliver the emf exactly as it’s made, flipping direction every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and we call the machine an alternator. Now the motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn, it cancels each flip the moment it happens, so the external current always runs one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. One fitting, two totally different outputs!
nye.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you work the crank yourself.
nye.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror sits at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and that commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction shows up on the exam just about every single year.
nye.25: Spin the coil at a perfectly steady rate and the induced emf traces out a sine wave — swinging positive, then negative, once per turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so that swing finishes 50 cycles every second: mains electricity at 50 Hz! We quote its size as 230 V, but that number hides a sneaky little detail — the voltage at the wall actually swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is just the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains where the name comes from.
nye.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and your wall, though, a physics problem rears its head! Wire has resistance, and current pushing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. And look closely — the quantity that gets squared is the current!
nye.27: Power equals voltage times current, so you can ship the same power either as a low voltage with a big current, or as a high voltage with a small current. Crank the voltage up tenfold and the current drops to a tenth — and the heating loss crashes to a hundredth! Over 170 km of line, high voltage isn’t a nice-to-have, it’s a flat-out necessity.
nye.28: And the gadget that makes voltage adjustable? It’s a grown-up version of Faraday’s iron ring — the transformer! Two coils share an iron core, and nothing moves at all. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core ferries it across to the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf over there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor — the power, ideally, unchanged!
nye.29: And this is exactly why the grid runs on AC: a transformer only works with changing flux! Feed it steady DC and the flux just sits there constant — nothing gets induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
nye.30: So the journey in Figure 1.1 is really just Faraday’s law, applied again and again! A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces about 20 kV; a step-up transformer hauls it up to 500 kV for the trip to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down in stages; and one last transformer near the house delivers 230 V to your wall — 50 times a second!
nye.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio, and the loss arithmetic all get built up properly in Transformers and transmission; and the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s toughest regular question — is waiting in Transmission of electricity.
nye.32: Now, one kind of generator on the grid breaks the spinning-coil mould entirely! More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that just sit dead still in the sun while current pours out of them. No coil, no magnet, no spinning — only electrons and light are on the move!
nye.33: Inside each cell is a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light comes in and knocks electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently right into the junction between the two slices — lets those loose electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way gate makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells make DC! A field is still doing the work here too — it’s just an electric field this time, parked at a junction instead of sweeping past a coil.
nye.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more piece of gear: the inverter, which converts the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design only asks you what an inverter does, not how it pulls it off — its electronic circuitry, just like the fine detail of the silicon, is beyond the course.
nye.35: One question’s still hanging: why on Earth should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? Answering that takes a brand-new idea about what light even is — that it shows up in discrete little packets of energy — and that’s the opening topic of Unit 4, where we’ll give those packets a name: photons!
nye.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; and the reason light can boot electrons out at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
nye.37: And that’s Area of Study 3, read through once, start to finish! Pass 2 tells it all over again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each one built for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the spinning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
nye.38: Pass 3 is your exam-prep zone: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with all the subtleties, the assessors’ advice, and exam-style questions worked right through. This Area of Study has pulled a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and the question types keep coming back the same; every one of them lives there.

munroe.1: On a winter evening, Melbourne switches on all at once: kettles, trams, lights, and every wall switch in the city. Pick one wire behind one switch and follow it backwards. It goes through the wall, under the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the edge of the city, then out along the 500 kV lines heading east across Gippsland. About 170 km from that Melbourne kitchen, the wire stops at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
munroe.2: The machine is a coil of wire spinning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley the spin comes from steam made by burning brown coal; in the Snowy Mountains, from falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, from moving air. Strip away the boilers and the blades and almost every large generator is the exact same thing: a coil of wire turning between magnets.
munroe.3: This Area of Study, called How are fields used in electricity generation?, explains that machine and the network bolted around it. It picks up exactly where the last page stopped: a field can be either static or changing, and the changing kind is the kind that makes electricity.
munroe.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the field distinction that actually matters. This Area of Study points that idea at the grid; Unit 4 points it, later, at light itself.
munroe.5: London, August 1831: Michael Faraday, who started out as a bookbinder's apprentice and went on to invent the field concept, had been chasing one hunch for ten years. A current makes a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted had shown in 1820. So, by symmetry, a magnetic field ought to make a current. Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, ran a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer—a sensitive current meter—hooked to the second, fully expecting the steady current the symmetry seemed to promise.
munroe.6: The needle did nothing, or close to nothing. At the instant he connected the battery, it kicked, then dropped back to zero and stayed there, no matter how strong and steady the current in the first coil was. At the instant he disconnected it, the needle kicked again, the other way. The effect he was waiting for never showed up; the only effect that did happen happened in the two brief moments when the magnetic field was changing.
munroe.7: By October he had boiled the experiment down to its plainest form. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle kicks. Hold the magnet still inside the coil—for as long as you like, with as strong a magnet as you like—and you get nothing. Pull it back out and the needle kicks the other way.
munroe.8: Moving in makes current one way; moving out makes current the other way; not moving makes no current. The field doesn't push charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
munroe.9: What a changing field does to charges is called an induced emf, short for electromotive force, which is a bad name on two counts: it is not a force, and it is measured in volts. The whole effect is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is just Faraday's trick done deliberately and on repeat. But saying “the field changes” is too vague to wire a country on. The real question is which quantity has to change.
munroe.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, complete with the full set of rules for what the meter does and doesn't register, opens Pass 2 at Faraday's law.
munroe.11: Think of the wire loop as a window frame and the magnetic field lines as threads poking through the opening. The thing Faraday's discovery actually hangs on is the magnetic flux: how much field gets through the loop. Hold the loop square-on to the field and the flux is biggest; tilt it and less gets through; turn it edge-on and the lines slide past without entering, so the flux is zero. The formula is ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet, the field strength times the area held perpendicular to it, and flux gets its own unit, the weber (Wb).
munroe.12: Since induction needs the flux to change, the formula is basically a list of things you can change. You can change B, by making the field stronger or weaker or by moving the loop nearer to or further from the magnet. You can change A, the area inside the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And you can change the angle, by spinning the loop so it faces the field, then shows its edge, then faces it again. Three knobs, then: field, area and angle.
munroe.13: Faraday's law says the induced emf equals how fast the flux is changing, times the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. In plain words, more flux change in less time gives a bigger emf, and a coil of N turns gets driven N times over. How big the flux is doesn't matter at all; only how fast it changes matters. The strongest field in the world, held perfectly still, induces nothing.
munroe.14: Of the three knobs, the grid turns the third one. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from maximum, through zero, to maximum the other way, and back again, which is change that never stops. This spinning coil is the basis of the rest of the page, with one exception saved for the end.
munroe.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change it are pinned down in Magnetic flux — the actual calculation for the perpendicular case, and the reasoning for every angle in between.
munroe.16: Faraday's law has a minus sign in it, and that minus sign has its own name: Lenz's law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current flows in whatever direction fights the change that created it. Push a magnet's north pole toward a coil and the induced current circles around so the coil shows a north pole right back, shoving against the approach; pull the magnet away and the current flips, now opposing the retreat. Every joule of electrical energy you induce has to be paid for as work done against the opposition.

bronowski.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields come to give us the electricity of our homes; and the whole explanation descends, remarkably, from a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. The story runs from Faraday's crowded bench all the way to the 500 kV lines striding out of the Latrobe Valley—and nothing here is settled, only followed.
bronowski.1: On a winter evening Melbourne calls upon its electricity: the kettles, the trams, the lights, the small click of a switch on a wall. Follow the wire behind that switch—back through the plaster, under the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city's edge, and then out along the 500 kV lines that march east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen, the wire comes to rest at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
bronowski.2: The machine is nothing more than a coil of wire turning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley it is steam, raised by burning brown coal, that turns it; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, the moving air. Strip away the boilers and the blades and almost every great generator is the same humble thing: a coil of wire spinning between magnets.
bronowski.3: This Area of Study, which asks How are fields used in electricity generation?, sets out to explain that machine and the vast network raised around it. And it begins exactly where the page before it left off: with the realisation that a field may be still or may be changing—and that it is the changing kind which gives us electricity.
bronowski.4: Connects The page before this one named static versus changing as the field distinction that matters most. This Area of Study carries that single idea into the grid; and Unit 4 will carry it further still, into the nature of light itself.
bronowski.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday—once a bookbinder's apprentice, and the man who first conceived of the field—had held to one conviction for a decade. An electric current makes a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted had shown in 1820; surely, then, a magnetic field ought to make a current. So Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer, a sensitive meter of current, joined to the second—expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
bronowski.6: The needle gave him nothing, or all but nothing. At the very instant the battery was joined, it flicked, then fell back to zero and stayed there—no matter how strong, how steady, the current in the first coil. And at the instant of breaking the circuit, it flicked once more, the other way. The effect he had looked for was not there at all; the effect that came was confined to those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
bronowski.7: By October he had pared the experiment down to its plainest form. Thrust a bar magnet into a coil of wire and the needle swings. Hold the magnet still inside the coil—hold it as long as you like, make it as strong as you like—and nothing flows. Draw it out again, and the needle swings the opposite way.
bronowski.8: Motion inward gives a current one way; motion outward gives it the other; no motion gives nothing at all. It is not the field that drives the charges through the wire. It is the change in the field.
bronowski.9: What a changing field does to charges we call an induced emf—short for electromotive force, a name history has left us that misleads on both counts: it is not a force, and we measure it in volts. The whole phenomenon is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page does nothing but repeat Faraday's effect, deliberately and over and over. Yet to say merely that the field changes is too loose a thing to build a grid upon. The real question is: which quantity must change?
bronowski.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full code of what registers on the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday's law.
bronowski.11: Think of the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as so many threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday's whole discovery turns is the magnetic flux—the amount of field threading the loop. Square-on to the field, the flux is greatest; tilt the loop, and less gets through; turn it edge-on, and the lines slip past without entering, and the flux falls to zero. The rule is written ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet—the strength of the field times the area held square to it—and flux is given a unit of its own, the weber (Wb).
bronowski.12: Since induction needs the flux to change, the formula itself names everything we are free to change. B we can change, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by carrying the loop nearer to or further from the magnet. A, the area within the field, we can change by sliding the loop in or out. And the angle we can change, by turning the loop until it faces the field, then turns its edge to it, then faces it once more: three things in all—field, area and angle.
bronowski.13: Faraday's law tells us that the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: more flux change in less time gives a larger emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. How great the flux is does not matter in the least; only how fast it changes. The mightiest field, held still, induces nothing.
bronowski.14: Of the three quantities, the grid leans on the third. The induced current flows in whatever direction opposes the very change that creates it. Bring a magnet's north pole toward a coil and the induced current circulates so the coil answers with a north pole of its own, pushing back against the approach; pull the magnet away and the current reverses, now opposing the retreat. bronowski.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and the wall, however, a difficulty of physics intrudes. Wire has resistance, and a current driven through resistance turns electrical energy into heat—a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. And the quantity that is squared is the current.
bronowski.27: Power is voltage times current, so the very same power may be sent as a low voltage with a great current, or as a high voltage with a small one. Raise the voltage tenfold and the current falls to a tenth—and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is no mere preference; it is a necessity.
bronowski.28: The device that lets us adjust the voltage is Faraday's iron ring grown up: the transformer. Two coils share a single iron core, and not a part of it moves. An alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries that flux on through the secondary coil; and there Faraday's law induces an emf, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns wound on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the very same factor, the power, ideally, untouched.
bronowski.29: And this, too, is why the grid lives on AC: a transformer works only with a changing flux. Steady DC leaves the flux constant; nothing is induced, and nothing comes out the far side.
bronowski.30: The journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is therefore Faraday's law applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces some 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the long carry to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations bring it back down by stages; and a last transformer near the house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times every second.
bronowski.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built up properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation—the hardest of the exam's regular questions—waits in Transmission of electricity.
bronowski.32: One kind of generator on the grid keeps to none of this turning-coil pattern. More than four million Australian rooftops now bear photovoltaic cells—panels that lie wholly still in the sun while current pours out of them. There is no coil, no magnet, no rotation at all; only electrons and light are in motion.
bronowski.33: Within each cell sits a thin sandwich of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light falling on it knocks electrons loose, and an electric field—built permanently into the junction between the two slices—lets those freed electrons cross one way only. A one-way action makes a one-way current: photovoltaic cells give DC. A field does the work here as everywhere—but now it is the electric field, sitting at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
bronowski.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more part: the inverter, which turns the panels' DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic workings, like the fine detail of the silicon, lie beyond the course.
bronowski.35: One question still stands: why light should be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all. The answer demands a new idea about the very nature of light—that it comes in discrete packets of energy—and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given their name: photons.
bronowski.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are treated in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4's photoelectric effect.
bronowski.37: Area of Study 3 has now been read once, entire. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday's law, Lenz's law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the rotating coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).

This Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam's 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring kinds of question are remarkably consistent; every one of them appears there.
