einstein.0: In this Area of Study I want to follow how fields give us the electricity in our walls; and the whole story, to my delight, descends from a single twitch of a meter needle in 1831. I shall trace it from Faraday’s little bench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley. I solve nothing here; I only walk the path.
einstein.1: Picture a winter evening in Melbourne, with the city drawing its electrical load: kettles, trams, lamps and the flick of a switch at home. Follow the wire behind that switch backwards in your mind — through the wall, beneath the street, through a suburban substation to a terminal station at the city’s edge, then out along the 500 kV lines that run east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen, the wire ends at a machine in the Latrobe Valley.
einstein.2: And what is that machine? Simply a coil of wire turning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal does the turning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, the moving air. Set aside the boilers and the blades, and nearly every great generator is the very same thing: a coil of wire spinning between magnets.
einstein.3: This Area of Study, which asks How are fields used in electricity generation?, sets out to explain that machine and the network we have built around it. It begins exactly where the last page left off: with the realisation that a field may be still or changing, and that it is the changing kind which gives us electricity.
einstein.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the field distinction that truly matters. Here I apply that idea to the grid; in Unit 4 I shall apply it once more, to light itself.
einstein.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, and the man who first imagined the field — had chased one conviction for ten years. An electric current makes a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted had shown in 1820; surely, then, a magnetic field ought to make a current. So Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive current meter — joined to the second, expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
einstein.6: Yet the needle showed nothing, or very nearly nothing: at the instant he connected the battery it gave a single kick, then fell back to zero and stayed there, no matter how strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant he disconnected it, the needle kicked again, the other way. The effect he had looked for was simply absent; the effect he saw happened only in those two fleeting moments when the magnetic field was changing.
einstein.7: By October he had pared the experiment down to its plainest form. Push a bar magnet into a coil of wire, and the needle deflects. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you like, with as strong a magnet as you like — and there is no current at all. Draw the magnet out again, and the needle deflects the other way.
einstein.8: Motion inward gives a current one way; motion outward, the other way; no motion, no current. The field itself does not drive the charges through the wire. The change in the field does. That, to me, is the whole secret.
einstein.9: What a changing field does to charges we call an induced emf, short for electromotive force — an unfortunate old name, for it is not a force at all, and we measure it in volts. The phenomenon as a whole is electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is simply Faraday’s effect, repeated on purpose, again and again. But to say merely that “the field changes” is too loose a thing to build a grid upon. The real question is: which quantity must change?
einstein.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full set of rules for what stirs the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
einstein.11: Let me picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as so many threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s discovery turns is the magnetic flux: how much field passes through the loop. Hold the loop square-on to the field and the flux is greatest; tilt it and less passes through; turn it edge-on and the lines slip past without entering at all, so the flux is zero. We write it ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength times the area held square to it — and flux has a unit of its own, the weber (Wb).
einstein.12: Since induction asks for changing flux, the formula itself lists what we are free to change. We may change B, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by carrying the loop nearer the magnet or further away. We may change A, the area lying within the field, by sliding the loop in or out. And we may change the angle, by turning the loop so it faces the field, then shows its edge, then faces it once more: three quantities in all — field, area and angle.
einstein.13: Faraday’s law tells us the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: more flux change in less time gives a larger emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. How much flux there is matters not at all; only how fast it changes. The mightiest field, held perfectly still, induces nothing.
einstein.14: Of the three quantities, the grid chooses the third. Spin a coil in a steady field and its flux swings from a maximum, through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back again — a change that never stops. This turning coil is the heart of all that follows, with one exception saved for the end.
einstein.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
einstein.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that little sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who stated it in 1834. The induced current always flows in whatever direction opposes the very change that created it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current circulates so the coil offers a north pole back, resisting the approach; pull the magnet away, and the current reverses, now opposing the retreat. The wording must be exact: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
einstein.17: Why must it oppose? Imagine, for a moment, the opposite. Were the induced current to help the change, a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, the flux would change faster, a larger current would follow and pull harder still — a runaway, making energy out of nothing from the slightest push. Nature forbids it, for energy is conserved; and that, in the end, is all Lenz’s law says. Every joule of electrical energy we induce must be paid for by work done against the opposition.
einstein.18: This is why a generator fights against being turned. A generator joined to nothing spins freely; connect a lamp and the turning grows harder, by just the energy the lamp burns. Connect a whole suburb, and it takes a power station’s worth of steam to keep the coil moving. An electric car braking turns this around: its motor acts as a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force itself, storing the energy in the battery instead of throwing it away as heat in the brake pads.
einstein.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping introduced in Position is stored energy.
einstein.20: Now I can look afresh at a machine we have already met. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor: a coil on an axle between the poles of a magnet; supply a current, and the two sides of the coil are pushed in opposite directions, so it turns, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn to keep the turning going one way.
einstein.21: Disconnect the battery and turn the coil by hand instead, and the turning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; and the emf drives a current out. The very same hardware is now a generator — the motor run backwards: a motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label on the motor diagram stays just as it was; only the direction in which the energy flows is reversed.
einstein.22: One design choice is left, namely how the turning coil hands its current to the still external circuit — and this is what sets the shape of the output. Slip rings, two complete rings each joined to one end of the coil, keep an unbroken contact and deliver the emf just as it is made, reversing every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and we call the machine an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the very opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it happens, so the outside current always flows the one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling but never reversing. One fitting, and two different outputs.
einstein.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you work the crank yourself.
einstein.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
einstein.25: When the coil turns at a perfectly even rate, the induced emf traces out a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once each turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so this swing completes 50 cycles every second — mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size is quoted as 230 V, but that number hides a subtlety: the voltage at the wall really swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 explains the name.
einstein.26: Between the Latrobe Valley and the wall, though, a problem of physics raises its head. Wire has resistance, and a current flowing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. Notice well what is squared: the current.
einstein.27: Power equals voltage times current, so the same power can be sent as a low voltage with a large current, or as a high voltage with a small one. Raise the voltage tenfold and the current falls to a tenth, while the heating loss falls to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is not a matter of taste but of necessity.
einstein.28: The device that lets us adjust the voltage is simply Faraday’s iron ring grown up: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core, and nothing whatever moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core carries it across to the secondary coil; and Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up and the current steps down by the same factor, the power, ideally, unchanged.
einstein.29: And here is why the grid must run on AC: a transformer works only with changing flux. With steady DC the flux holds constant; nothing is induced, and nothing comes out the other side.
einstein.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is nothing but Faraday’s law, applied over and over. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces some 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the trip to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations bring it back down in stages; and a last transformer near the house hands 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second.
einstein.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
einstein.32: One kind of generator on the grid does not follow the turning-coil pattern at all. More than four million Australian rooftops now carry photovoltaic cells — panels that lie perfectly still in the sun while current flows out of them. There is no coil, no magnet, no turning; only the electrons and the light are in motion.
einstein.33: Inside each cell sits a thin layer of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Light falling on it jostles electrons loose, and an electric field — built permanently into the junction between the two slices — lets those loosened electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way action gives a one-way current: photovoltaic cells make DC. A field is still doing the work, only now it is the electric field, sitting at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
einstein.34: Houses and the grid run on AC, so every rooftop system carries one more part: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic circuitry, like the fine workings of the silicon, lies beyond this course.
einstein.35: One question still hangs in the air: why should light be able to knock electrons out of silicon at all? The answer calls for a new idea about the very nature of light — that it arrives in discrete little packets of energy — and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given the name photons.
einstein.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
einstein.37: Area of Study 3 has now been read once, in full. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the turning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).

attenborough.0: Area of Study 3 asks how fields conjure the mains electricity all around us — and the whole sweeping story descends from a single, almost imperceptible twitch of a meter needle in 1831. We shall follow the thread from Faraday’s cluttered bench all the way to the 500 kV lines marching out of the Latrobe Valley. Nothing here is solved; it is only traced.
attenborough.1: On a winter evening, Melbourne stirs and draws its electrical load — kettles, trams, lamps, the quiet flick of a switch. Follow the wire behind that switch backwards: through the wall, beneath the street, into a suburban substation, on to a terminal station at the city’s edge, then out along the 500 kV lines that run east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen, the wire comes at last to rest — at a single machine in the Latrobe Valley.
attenborough.2: And that machine is nothing more than a coil of wire, turning in a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley, steam from burning brown coal sets it spinning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, the moving air itself. Strip away the boilers and the blades, and almost every great generator is one and the same creature: a coil of wire, turning patiently between magnets.
attenborough.3: This Area of Study, How are fields used in electricity generation?, is the tale of that device and the vast web built around it. It opens exactly where the last page closed — with the quiet observation that a field may be still, or it may be changing, and that it is the changing kind, and only the changing kind, that brings electricity into being.
attenborough.4: Connects The previous page singled out static versus changing as the one classification of fields that truly matters. Here we set that idea loose upon the grid; and in Unit 4 it returns once more, to work its magic upon light itself.
attenborough.5: London, August 1831. Michael Faraday — once a bookbinder’s apprentice, now the father of the field — had chased a single conviction for a decade. An electric current breathes a magnetic field into the world, as Hans Christian Oersted had revealed in 1820. Surely, then, a magnetic field must give birth to a current in return. So Faraday wound two separate coils onto an iron ring, sent a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a delicate meter of current — wired to the second, expecting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
attenborough.6: The needle gave nothing — or almost nothing. At the very instant the battery touched, it flicked, then sank back to zero and lay still, no matter how strong, how steady the current in the first coil. And at the instant of disconnection, it flicked once more — the other way. The effect he had awaited never came; the effect he saw lived only in those two fleeting moments, when the magnetic field was changing.
attenborough.7: By October he had pared the experiment down to its purest form. Slide a bar magnet into a coil of wire, and the needle leaps. Hold the magnet still inside the coil — for as long as you please, with as mighty a magnet as you like — and nothing stirs. Draw it out again, and the needle leaps the opposite way.
attenborough.8: Motion inward sends current one way; motion outward sends it the other; stillness sends nothing at all. It is not the field that drives the charges through the wire. It is the change in the field.
attenborough.9: The push a changing field works upon charges is called an induced emf — short for electromotive force, a misleading old name, for it is no force at all, and it is measured in volts. The whole phenomenon we call electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page is simply Faraday’s effect, summoned deliberately and tirelessly. But to say merely that “the field changes” is far too loose a thing to build a grid upon. The question is: which quantity must change?
attenborough.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment — with its full set of rules for what registers on the meter and what does not — opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
attenborough.11: Picture the wire loop as a window frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads passing through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s whole discovery turns is the magnetic flux — the amount of field threading through that loop. Held square-on to the field, the flux is greatest; tilt it, and less slips through; turn it edge-on, and the lines glide past without entering at all, and the flux falls to zero. The formula bears the name ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength, multiplied by the area held perpendicular to it — and flux has a unit all its own, the weber (Wb).
attenborough.12: Because induction demands changing flux, the formula itself lays out everything we may change. B we may change — by strengthening or weakening the field, or by carrying the loop nearer to, or further from, the magnet. A, the area within the field, we may change by sliding the loop in or out. And the angle we may change, by turning the loop so it faces the field, then offers its edge, then faces it once more. Three quantities, then: field, area and angle.
attenborough.13: Faraday’s law tells us the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns on the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Put plainly: more flux change in less time gives a greater emf, and a coil of N turns feels the push N times over. The sheer size of the flux means nothing; only the speed of its change matters. The mightiest field in the world, held perfectly still, induces nothing at all.
attenborough.14: Of the three quantities, the grid reaches for the third. When a coil turns in a steady field, its flux swings from a maximum, down through zero, to a maximum the other way, and back again — a change that never ceases. This turning coil is the heart of all that follows, save for a single exception, waiting at the very end.
attenborough.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made precise in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, qualitative reasoning for every angle in between.
attenborough.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign — and that small minus sign has a name all its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who declared it in 1834. The induced current always flows in the direction that opposes the very change that creates it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current wheels round so the coil turns a north pole back upon it, resisting the approach; withdraw the magnet, and the current reverses, now opposing the retreat. The wording is exact: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
attenborough.17: Why opposition, and never aid? Consider the alternative. If the induced current helped the change along, a nudged magnet would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a larger current, drawing it harder still — a runaway, conjuring energy from a single gentle push, out of nothing. Nature forbids it; the conservation of energy forbids it. And that is the deep meaning of Lenz’s law: it is energy conservation itself, in disguise. Every joule of electrical energy induced must be paid for — as work done against the opposition.
attenborough.18: This is why a generator fights against being turned. Connected to nothing, it spins freely; connect a single lamp, and the turning grows harder, the very weight of the energy the lamp drinks down. Connect a whole suburb, and it takes a power station’s torrent of steam to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car turns the whole thing on its head: its motor becomes a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force itself — storing the energy away in the battery, rather than squandering it as heat in the brake pads.
attenborough.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they protect is the same energy bookkeeping introduced in Position is stored energy.
attenborough.20: A machine we have already met may now be seen anew. Area of Study 2 introduced the DC motor — a coil on an axle, slung between magnet poles. Send current through it, and the two sides of the coil are pushed in opposite directions, and round it turns, while a split-ring commutator flips the current every half-turn, so the rotation never falters in its direction.
attenborough.21: But disconnect the battery and turn the coil by hand, and watch what happens: the turning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The very same hardware now works as a generator — the motor run in reverse. A motor drinks electrical energy to make motion; a generator drinks motion to make electrical energy. Every single label on the motor diagram stays exactly as it was; only the direction of the energy’s flow has turned about.
attenborough.22: One choice of design remains — how the turning coil hands its current across to the still external circuit — and it decides the very form of the output. Slip rings — two complete rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep an unbroken contact and deliver the emf just as it is born, reversing every half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is called an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does precisely the opposite: by swapping the connections every half-turn it cancels each reversal as it comes, so the external current always flows one way — direct current, DC, rising and falling, but never reversing. One fitting, two utterly different outputs.
attenborough.23: Simulation 1.1 lets you turn the crank yourself.
attenborough.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
attenborough.25: When the coil turns at a perfectly even pace, the induced emf traces a sine wave — swinging positive, then negative, once with every turn, as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so the swing completes 50 cycles every second: mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size is quoted as 230 V — but that figure hides a subtlety. The voltage at the wall in truth swings between roughly +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the very same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 unravels the name.
attenborough.26: Yet between the Latrobe Valley and the wall, a problem of physics rears its head. Wire has resistance, and current flowing through resistance spills electrical energy away as heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. And the quantity that is squared is the current.
attenborough.27: Power equals voltage multiplied by current — so the very same power may be carried as a low voltage with a great current, or a high voltage with a small one. Raise the voltage tenfold, and the current falls to a tenth, and the heating loss to a hundredth. Across 170 km of line, high voltage is no mere preference; it is a necessity.
attenborough.28: The device that makes voltage pliable is Faraday’s iron ring, grown to maturity: the transformer. Two coils share a single iron core, and not one part of it moves. Alternating current in the primary coil breeds a changing flux; the core carries that flux through the secondary coil; and there Faraday’s law induces an emf, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns on each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. Step the voltage up, and the current steps down by the very same factor, the power, ideally, untouched.
attenborough.29: And here, too, is why the grid lives on AC: a transformer wakes only to changing flux. With steady DC, the flux lies still; nothing is induced, and nothing at all emerges at the output.
attenborough.30: So the journey drawn in Figure 1.1 is Faraday’s law, applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces some 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the long passage to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations bring it back down by stages; and one last transformer, near the house, delivers 230 V to the wall — 50 times every second.
attenborough.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
attenborough.32: One kind of generator on the grid breaks the turning-coil mould entirely. More than four million Australian rooftops now bear photovoltaic cells — panels that lie utterly still in the sun, while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no turning of any kind; only electrons and light are in motion.
attenborough.33: Within each cell lies a thin layer — two slices of silicon, each coaxed to conduct a little differently. Incoming light knocks electrons loose, and an electric field, built permanently into the junction between the two slices, lets those freed electrons cross in one direction only. A one-way passage gives a one-way current: photovoltaic cells produce DC. A field still does the work — the electric field, this time, lodged at a junction rather than sweeping past a coil.
attenborough.34: Houses and the grid live on AC, so every rooftop system carries one further piece: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its electronic innards, like the fine workings of the silicon, lie beyond the course.
attenborough.35: One question lingers: why should light be able to knock electrons free from silicon at all? The answer calls for a wholly new idea about the very nature of light — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening topic of Unit 4, where those packets are given a name: photons.
attenborough.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
attenborough.37: And so Area of Study 3 has now been read once, in full. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each one meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the turning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic).
attenborough.38: Pass 3 is for the exam: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity, for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity, for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked through in full. This Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring question types are faithful and consistent; every last one of them appears there.

greekepic.0: Sing, Muse, of how the fields beget the mains-borne fire that lights a city: for Area of Study 3 traces this lineage, and the whole tale descends from a single trembling of a meter’s needle in the year 1831. greekepic.1: On a winter’s evening Melbourne draws upon her electrical hoard: the kettles, the trams, the lamps, the household switches thrown by mortal hands. Yet set aside the boilers and the blades, and nearly every great generator is the selfsame thing: a coil of wire turning between magnets.

greekepic.5: London, in the August of 1831: Michael Faraday, once a bookbinder’s apprentice and the begetter of the field-idea, had pursued one conviction for the span of a decade. Faraday wound two sundered coils upon an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a sensitive meter of current — joined to the second, looking for the steady current that symmetry seemed to foretell.
greekepic.6: Yet the needle declared nothing, or near to nothing: in the instant the battery was joined, it leapt aside, then fell back to zero and there abided, however strong and however steady the current ran in the first coil. In the instant of parting, it leapt once more, and in the opposite quarter. The foretold effect was nowhere; the effect that came came only in those two brief moments when the magnetic field was changing.
greekepic.7: By October the trial had been pared to its plainest shape. Drive a bar magnet into a coil of wire, and the needle leaps aside. Hold the magnet still within the coil, for any span and with any might of magnet, and no current comes. Draw it forth again, and the needle leaps aside in the opposite quarter.
greekepic.8: Motion inward begets a current in one direction; motion outward begets it in the other; no motion begets nothing. The field does not drive the charges through the wire. The change in the field does.
greekepic.9: The deed that a changing field works upon charges is named an induced emf, short for electromotive force — a name handed down to mislead, for it is no force at all, and it is measured in volts. The whole of the wonder is electromagnetic induction, and every generator upon this page reckons Faraday’s effect anew, on purpose and without end. Yet to say merely that the field changes is no firm ground on which to raise a grid. The question is which quantity must change.
greekepic.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil trial, with the whole array of decrees for what the meter declares and what it does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
greekepic.11: Behold the wire loop as a window-frame, and the magnetic field lines as threads that pass through the opening. The quantity on which Faraday’s discovery hangs is the magnetic flux: the measure of field that passes through the loop. When the loop stands square to the field the flux is greatest; when it tilts, less passes through; when it stands edge-on, the lines slip past without entering, and the flux is naught. The formula bears the name ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength multiplied by the area held square to it — and the flux has its own unit, the weber (Wb).
greekepic.12: Since induction craves a changing flux, the formula sets forth the quantities that may be changed. B may be changed, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by bearing the loop nearer to the magnet or farther from it. A, the area within the field, may be changed by sliding the loop in or out. The angle may be changed, by turning the loop so that it faces the field, then turns its edge, then faces it once more: three quantities, namely field, area and angle.
greekepic.13: Faraday’s law decrees that the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns upon the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. The magnitude of the flux counts for nothing; only its rate of change counts at all. The mightiest field, held still, induces nothing.
greekepic.14: Of the three quantities, the grid takes the third. When a coil wheels within a steady field its flux swings from greatest, through naught, to greatest in the opposite quarter, and back again, begetting a change that never ceases. This wheeling coil is the foundation of all that the song still has to tell, save one exception at the end.
greekepic.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made exact in Magnetic flux — reckoning for the perpendicular case, and reasoning by sight for every angle that lies between.
greekepic.16: Faraday’s law bears a minus sign, and that minus sign has a name of its own: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who proclaimed it in the year 1834. The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the change that begot it. Bear a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current circles so that the coil turns a north pole back in answer, fighting the approach; draw the magnet forth, and the current reverses, and now opposes the withdrawal. The wording is exact: the current opposes the change in flux, never the field itself.
greekepic.17: The need for this opposition is laid bare by weighing the alternative. Were the induced current to aid the change, a magnet once stirred would be drawn in the faster, changing the flux the faster, inducing a greater current and drawing it harder yet — a runaway begetting energy from no source at all, set loose by the lightest push. Energy conservation forbids it, and that is the very content of Lenz’s law: it is a consequence of energy conservation. greekepic.18: This is why a generator fights its own turning. A generator joined to nothing wheels freely; join a lamp, and the turning grows harder, mirroring the energy the lamp devours. Join a suburb, and a power station’s outpouring of steam is needed to keep the coil wheeling on. A braking electric car turns the order about: its motor takes up the work of a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, hoarding energy in the battery rather than wasting it as heat in the brake pads.
greekepic.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, dwell at Lenz’s law. The ledger they keep is the same energy bookkeeping first set forth in Position is stored energy.
greekepic.20: A machine once studied may now be looked upon anew. Area of Study 2 brought forth the DC motor: a coil upon an axle between magnet poles; with current supplied, the two sides of the coil are thrust in opposite directions, begetting rotation, while a split-ring commutator reverses the current every half-turn so that the rotation does not turn back.
greekepic.21: Unjoin the battery and turn the coil by hand: the turning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current forth. The selfsame hardware now bears the office of a generator, the mirror of the motor: a motor devours electrical energy to beget motion; a generator devours motion to beget electrical energy. Every label upon the motor diagram abides unchanged; only the direction of the energy’s flow is reversed.
greekepic.22: One choice of design abides, namely how the wheeling coil hands its current to the still circuit beyond, and this choice decrees the form of the output. Slip rings — two whole rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep unbroken contact and deliver the emf as it is begotten, reversing direction every half-turn. One fitting yields two sundered outputs.
greekepic.23: Simulation 1.1 lets the crank be worked by your own hand.
greekepic.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror dwells at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is weighed almost every year.
greekepic.25: When the coil wheels at a perfectly steady rate the induced emf traces a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once each turn as the flux rises and falls. Its magnitude is named as 230 V, yet that figure hides a subtlety: the voltage at the wall in truth swings between near +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 declares the name.

greekepic.27: Power equals voltage multiplied by current, so the same power may be borne as a low voltage with a great current, or as a high voltage with a small current. greekepic.28: The device that renders voltage adjustable is Faraday’s iron ring grown to its full form: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core; no part moves. Alternating current in the primary coil raises a changing flux; the core bears it through the secondary coil; Faraday’s law induces an emf there, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns upon each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. When the voltage is stepped up the current steps down by the selfsame factor, the power, in the ideal, abiding unchanged.
greekepic.29: This also declares why the grid runs upon AC: a transformer works only with changing flux. With steady DC the flux abides constant; nothing is induced, and nothing is begotten at the output.
greekepic.30: The journey shown in Figure 1.1 is therefore Faraday’s law worked again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces near 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the passage to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down by stages; and a last transformer near the house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times every second.

More than four million Australian rooftops now bear photovoltaic cells, panels that abide motionless in the sun while current flows forth from them. There is no coil, no magnet and no turning; only electrons and light are in motion.

cnpop.0: 研究领域 3 要扒的，是场到底怎么变出咱家墙上插座里的电；而这整套解释，全是从 1831 年一根仪表指针的一次轻轻抖动顺藤摸瓜下来的。故事从法拉第的实验台一路拉到离开拉特罗布河谷的 500 kV 高压线——这里啥都不解题，只负责把这条线捋清楚。
cnpop.1: 冬天的傍晚，墨尔本开始疯狂吸电：烧水壶、电车、灯、还有你家随手一摁的开关。开关后面那根电线，你可以一路往回追——穿墙、钻到马路底下、过郊区变电站、到城市边上的终端站，再顺着往东横穿吉普斯兰的 500 kV 高压线一路狂奔。从墨尔本的厨房算起，大约 170 km 之外，这根线终于撞上了拉特罗布河谷里的一台机器。
cnpop.2: 这台机器，说白了就是一圈在磁场里打转的线圈。在拉特罗布河谷，烧褐煤产生的蒸汽负责让它转；在雪山，是落下来的水；在墨尔本以西的平原上，是流动的空气。但你把锅炉和叶片这些花架子撇开，几乎每一台大型发电机本质上都是同一个玩意儿：一圈在磁铁之间转来转去的线圈。
cnpop.3: 这个研究领域的标题是 How are fields used in electricity generation?，要讲的就是这台机器，以及围着它搭起来的整张电网。它接着上一页的结尾往下说：场可以是静的，也可以是变的，而能变出电的，正是那个会变的。
cnpop.4: Connects 上一页已经点明，场最要命的分类就是 static versus changing。这个研究领域把这个想法套到电网上；到了 Unit 4，同一招还会再用一次——这次对象换成光本身。
cnpop.5: 伦敦，1831 年 8 月：迈克尔·法拉第，一个装订工出身、又亲手发明了“场”这个概念的人，整整十年死磕一个信念。电流能造出磁场，这事 1820 年汉斯·克里斯蒂安·奥斯特已经证给大家看了。那按对称性反推，磁场也该能造出电流啊。于是法拉第把两圈互不相连的线圈缠在一个铁环上，给第一圈通上稳稳的电流，盯着接在第二圈上的电流计——一种超灵敏的电流表——就等着那个对称性预言里该出现的稳定电流。
cnpop.6: 结果指针啥也没显示，或者说几乎啥也没显示：就在电池接通的那一刹那，它抖了一下，然后回到零，老老实实趴在那儿不动，第一圈里的电流再强再稳都不带搭理你的。等到断开的那一刹那，它又抖了一下——这回往反方向抖。预想中的效果一点没有；真正出现的效果，只发生在磁场正在变化的那两个一闪而过的瞬间。
cnpop.7: 到了十月，这个实验已经被精简到最干净利落的形态。把一根条形磁铁往线圈里推，指针就偏。让磁铁稳稳停在线圈里头——停多久、磁铁多强都一样——一点电流都没有。把它抽出来，指针又偏，方向反过来。
cnpop.8: 往里动，产生一个方向的电流；往外动，产生另一个方向的电流；不动，啥都没有。驱动电荷在导线里跑的，不是场本身。是场的变化。
cnpop.9: 变化的场对电荷使的这股劲儿，叫感应电动势（emf，electromotive force 的缩写）——这是个相当误导人的历史名字：它根本不是一种力，而且单位是伏特。整个现象统称电磁感应，而这一页上的每一台发电机，干的都是把法拉第这个效应有意地、不停地复刻一遍。不过光说“场在变”，还不够精确到能拿来撑起一整张电网。真正的问题是：到底哪个量得变。
cnpop.10: Connects 磁铁加线圈的实验，连同那一整套“什么会让表动、什么不会”的规则，是 Pass 2 开篇 Faraday's law 的内容。
cnpop.11: 你可以把这圈导线想成一个窗框，把磁场线想成一根根穿过窗洞的线。法拉第这个发现真正依赖的那个量，就是磁通量：穿过这个圈的场到底有多少。线圈正对着场时，通量最大；歪一点，穿过的就少；侧着脸对场时，磁场线擦边而过、压根没进来，通量就是零。它的公式叫 ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet，也就是场强乘上垂直于它摆放的面积，而通量还有自己专属的单位——韦伯（Wb）。
cnpop.12: 既然感应靠的是通量变化，那这个公式就等于把所有能下手去改的量都列出来了。B 可以改，要么把场调强调弱，要么把圈挪近或挪远磁铁。A，也就是落在场里那块面积，可以靠把圈滑进滑出来改。角度也能改，把圈转一转，先正对着场、再侧着脸、然后又正对回来：一共三个量——场、面积、角度。
cnpop.13: 法拉第定律说的是：感应电动势等于通量变化的速率，再乘上线圈的匝数 ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet。翻成人话就是，越短的时间里通量变得越多，电动势就越大，而 N 匝的线圈相当于把这股劲儿走了 N 遍。通量本身有多大根本不重要；唯一重要的是它变化的快慢。最强的场，只要你稳着不动，感应出来的就是个寂寞。
cnpop.14: 三个量里头，电网挑的是第三个。当一个线圈在稳稳的场里转起来，它的通量就会从最大，过零，冲到另一个方向的最大，再荡回来——变化停不下来。这个旋转的线圈，就是接下来整段故事的主心骨，只在结尾留了一个例外。
cnpop.15: Connects 通量、韦伯，以及改它的那三种办法，会在 Magnetic flux 里讲精确——垂直情形要算出来，中间各种角度则靠定性推理。
cnpop.16: 法拉第定律里带个负号，而这个负号有它自己的名字：楞次定律，得名于海因里希·楞次，他在 1834 年把它说出来的。感应电流流动的方向，永远是去对抗那个产生它的变化。把磁铁的 N 极往线圈推，感应出来的电流就会绕成让线圈也亮出一个 N 极来怼回去，抵抗你靠近；把磁铁抽走，电流反过来，转头去对抗你这一抽。这措辞得抠字眼：电流对抗的是通量的变化，不是场本身。
cnpop.17: 这种“对抗”为啥是必须的，反过来想想另一种可能就懂了。假如感应电流是去帮着这个变化，那一根被挪动的磁铁就会被吸得更快，通量变得更快，感应出更大的电流，再把它吸得更狠——一个轻轻一推就停不下来的失控过程，凭空不花一分钱就源源不断造出能量。能量守恒明令禁止这事，而这就是楞次定律的全部内涵：它是能量守恒的一个推论。每一焦耳被感应出来的电能，都必须靠你克服那股“对抗”做的功来支付。
cnpop.18: 这也正是发电机为啥会抗着不让你转。一台啥都没接的发电机，转得轻飘飘；一接上灯，转起来就费劲了，费的那点劲儿正好对应灯吃掉的能量。要是接上一整个郊区，那就得靠一座电站源源不断的蒸汽，才能把线圈转下去。一辆刹车的电动车把这套反着用：它的电机这会儿当发电机使，楞次的那股“对抗”就变成了刹车力，把能量存进电池里，而不是白白在刹车片上烧成热。
cnpop.19: Connects 那套定方向的规则，连同“三步保你不搞反”的口诀，都在 Lenz's law 里。它们守护的那本账，正是 Position is stored energy 里介绍过的同一本能量账。
cnpop.20: 之前学过的一台机器，现在可以拿回来重新审视一遍。研究领域 2 介绍过直流电动机：磁极之间一根轴上的线圈；一通电，线圈两边就被往相反方向推，转起来，而一个分裂环换向器每转半圈就把电流方向倒一次，好让旋转方向不至于跟着乱掉。
cnpop.21: 要是把电池拔了、改用手去转线圈，旋转就会改变穿过线圈的通量；变化的通量感应出电动势；电动势把电流往外赶。同一套硬件这会儿摇身一变成了发电机，正好是电动机的反向操作：电动机是吃电能吐运动；发电机是吃运动吐电能。电动机示意图上的每一个标签都原封不动；唯一掉头的，是能量流动的方向。
cnpop.22: 还剩一个设计上的抉择，就是旋转的线圈到底怎么把电流交给外头那个不动的电路——而这一抉择，直接决定了输出长啥样。滑环，也就是两个完整的环、各接线圈的一头，能保持连续接触、电动势造出来啥样就给你送啥样，每转半圈方向倒一次。这种输出就是交流电（AC），这机器就叫交流发电机。电动机自带的那个零件——分裂环换向器——干的恰好相反：它每半圈换一次接线，把每一次反向当场抵消掉，于是外电路里的电流永远朝同一个方向流，也就是直流电（DC），有起有伏，但绝不掉头。同一个零件，硬是整出两种不同的输出。
cnpop.23: Simulation 1.1 让你可以亲手摇这个曲柄。
cnpop.24: Connects 这面镜子的电动机那一半在 The DC motor；发电机那一半，连同两种端头零件，塞满了 Generators and alternators——而换向器对滑环这个区别，几乎年年考。
cnpop.25: 当线圈以一个稳到完美的速率旋转，感应电动势画出来的就是一条正弦波，随着通量起起落落，每转一圈就先正后负地荡一回。澳大利亚的电网被调到这个荡动每秒钟正好完成 50 个周期，市电 50 Hz。它的大小被标成 230 V，可这个数字藏着个小心机：墙上插座的电压其实是在大约 +325 V 和 −325 V 之间来回荡的，而 230 V 是那个能送出同样功率的稳定直流电压。这个等效平均值，就叫均方根（rms）值；名字咋来的，Pass 2 会解释。
cnpop.26: 可问题来了：从拉特罗布河谷到你家墙上，中间冒出来一个物理难题。导线有电阻，电流流过电阻就把电能变成热白白损耗掉，这笔损耗写出来是 Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet。注意看，被平方的那个量，是电流。
cnpop.27: 功率等于电压乘电流，所以同样一份功率，你可以用低电压配大电流来送，也可以用高电压配小电流来送。把电压抬高到十倍，电流就降到十分之一，发热损耗直接砍到百分之一。送 170 km 这么远，高压可不是“偏好”，是“没得选”。
cnpop.28: 让电压能随意调高调低的那个家伙，是法拉第那个铁环进化出来的版本：变压器。两圈线圈共用一个铁芯；没有任何东西在动。初级线圈里的交流电产生变化的通量；铁芯把它带过次级线圈；法拉第定律就在那儿感应出一个电动势，按两边的匝数精确成比例地升上去或降下来 N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet。电压升上去的时候，电流就按同样的倍数降下来，理想情况下功率纹丝不动。
cnpop.29: 这也顺便解释了电网为啥跑交流：变压器只有在通量变化时才工作。换成稳稳的直流，通量恒定不变；啥都感应不出来，输出端也就啥都没有。
cnpop.30: 所以 Figure 1.1 里画的这趟旅程，本质上就是法拉第定律被反反复复地套用。拉特罗布河谷的一圈线圈感应出大约 20 kV；一台升压变压器把它抬到 500 kV 送往墨尔本；终端站和郊区变电站再一级一级地把它压回去；最后房子附近的一台变压器，把 230 V 送到墙上，一秒钟 50 次。
cnpop.31: Connects 均方根值、变压器比和损耗那笔账，会在 Transformers and transmission 里好好搭起来；而整套供电系统的计算——考试里最硬的那道常客题——在 Transmission of electricity 等着你。
cnpop.32: 电网上有一类发电机，并不走旋转线圈这套模板。如今澳大利亚四百多万个屋顶上都驮着光伏电池，这些板子稳稳地晒着太阳一动不动，电就从里头哗哗往外流。没有线圈，没有磁铁，也没有旋转；动的只有电子和光。
cnpop.33: 每片电池里头，是薄薄一层的两片硅，各自经过处理、导电方式略有不同。射进来的光把电子撞离原位，而一个永久内建在两片之间结上的电场，只允许这些被撞出来的电子朝一个方向越过去。一个单向的动作产生一股单向的电流：光伏电池产生的是直流电。干活的依旧是场，只不过这回是电场，呆在一个结上，而不是从一圈线圈旁边掠过。
cnpop.34: 房子和电网跑的都是交流，所以每套屋顶系统都还得配一个零件：逆变器，把板子产生的直流转成 230 V 交流。学习设计只要求你知道逆变器干啥，不要求知道它咋干的；它的电子电路，跟硅片内部的详细运作一样，超出课程范围。
cnpop.35: 还剩一个问题：光凭啥就能把电子从硅里撞出来？答案需要一个关于光本质的全新想法——光是一包一包离散的能量送过来的，而这正是 Unit 4 的开篇主题，到那儿这些“能量包”会有个名字，叫光子。
cnpop.36: Connects 电池和它的逆变器在 Photovoltaic cells 里讲；光到底凭啥能把电子打出来，则是 Unit 4 的 photoelectric effect。
cnpop.37: 研究领域 3 到这儿就完完整整读过一遍了。Pass 2 会把它当核心内容重讲一遍，分成编号 6 到 8 的三章，每一章都是给你上课前一晚临阵磨枪用的：电磁感应（通量、法拉第定律、楞次定律以及它们的图像）；发电机和交流发电机（旋转线圈、两种端头零件、光伏）；变压器和输电（均方根值、变压器比、损耗那笔账）。
cnpop.38: Pass 3 是备考用的：按学习设计顺序排两章——发电对应知识点 KK 3.1 到 3.4，输电对应 KK 3.5 到 3.8——那些细枝末节、评卷人的提醒、还有考试风格的题目全都完整地做了一遍。这个研究领域在最近每一份卷子上都稳稳地值 22 分（总分 120），而且常考题型相当固定；它们全都出现在那儿。
cnpop.39: Connects Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction 是下一章；Pass 3 · Generation of electricity 装的是这块内容的备考。

confucius-en.0: The Master said. Area of Study 3 inquires how fields bring forth the mains electricity of a city; and the whole account descends from a single trembling of a meter’s needle in 1831. From Faraday’s bench it reaches to the 500 kV lines departing the Latrobe Valley. Here nothing is concluded; the path is only traced, that the student may walk it.
confucius-en.1: On a winter evening Melbourne draws its load: the kettle, the tram, the lamp, the switch in the home. Follow the wire behind that switch backward — through the wall, beneath the street, through a suburban substation to a terminal station at the city’s edge, and onward along the 500 kV lines that run east across Gippsland. Some 170 km from a Melbourne kitchen the wire ends at a machine in the Latrobe Valley. The humble switch and the distant machine are joined as parent and child.
confucius-en.2: What is this machine? A coil of wire turning within a magnetic field. In the Latrobe Valley steam from burning brown coal gives the turning; in the Snowy Mountains, falling water; on the plains west of Melbourne, moving air. Set aside the boilers and the blades, and nearly every great generator is the selfsame thing: a coil of wire turning between magnets. Many appearances, one principle — the wise look past the form to the thing itself.
confucius-en.3: This Area of Study, named How are fields used in electricity generation?, expounds that device and the network raised around it. It begins where the former page left off: with the teaching that a field may be still or changing, and that the changing kind is the kind which gives electricity.
confucius-en.4: Connects The former page named static versus changing as the field’s weightiest distinction. This Area of Study sets that idea upon the grid; and Unit 4 sets it down once more, upon light itself.
confucius-en.5: The Master said. London, August of 1831: Michael Faraday, once a bookbinder’s apprentice and father of the field, had held to one conviction for ten years. A current makes a magnetic field, as Hans Christian Oersted showed in 1820; therefore, by right reasoning, a magnetic field ought to make a current. So Faraday wound two coils upon an iron ring, drove a steady current through the first, and watched a galvanometer — a delicate meter of current — joined to the second, awaiting the steady current that symmetry seemed to promise.
confucius-en.6: The needle gave back nothing, or near nothing. At the instant the battery was joined it leapt, then fell to zero and rested there, however strong and steady the current in the first coil. At the instant of parting it leapt again, the opposite way. The looked-for effect did not come; the effect that came appeared only in those two brief moments when the field was changing. He who expects much and observes faithfully is corrected by the world.
confucius-en.7: By October the experiment was pared to its plainest shape. Thrust a bar magnet into a coil, and the needle stirs. Hold the magnet still within the coil — for any length of time, with any strength — and no current comes. Draw it out, and the needle stirs the opposite way.
confucius-en.8: Motion inward gives current one way; motion outward, the other; no motion, none at all. The field does not drive the charges through the wire. The change in the field does. Therein lies the whole lesson.
confucius-en.9: The action a changing field works upon charges is named an induced emf — short for electromotive force, an ill-chosen name handed down to us: it is no force, and it is measured in volts. The whole matter is called electromagnetic induction, and every generator on this page repeats Faraday’s effect by design and without ceasing. Yet to say only that the field changes is too loose a foundation for a grid. The proper question is: which quantity must change?
confucius-en.10: Connects The magnet-and-coil experiment, with its full rule of what registers upon the meter and what does not, opens Pass 2 at Faraday’s law.
confucius-en.11: Regard the wire loop as a window frame, and the field lines as threads passing through its opening. The quantity upon which Faraday’s discovery rests is the magnetic flux: the measure of field passing through the loop. Held square-on to the field, the flux is greatest; tilted, less passes through; held edge-on, the lines pass by without entering, and the flux is zero. The formula bears the name ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet — the field strength multiplied by the area held perpendicular to it; and flux has its own measure, the weber (Wb).
confucius-en.12: Since induction asks for changing flux, the formula sets forth the quantities one may change. B may be changed, by strengthening or weakening the field, or by bearing the loop nearer or further from the magnet. A, the area within the field, may be changed by sliding the loop in or out. The angle may be changed, by turning the loop so that it faces the field, then offers its edge, then faces it again. Three quantities in all: field, area and angle.
confucius-en.13: Faraday’s law teaches that the induced emf equals the rate at which the flux changes, multiplied by the number of turns upon the coil: ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet. Said plainly, more flux change in less time gives a greater emf, and a coil of N turns is driven N times over. The size of the flux is of no account; only the swiftness of its change. The strongest field, held still, induces nothing — as a teacher of great learning who never speaks instructs no one.
confucius-en.14: Of the three quantities, the grid takes the third. When a coil turns in a steady field, its flux swings from greatest, through zero, to greatest the other way, and back — a change that does not cease. This turning coil is the foundation of all that remains, save one matter kept for the end.
confucius-en.15: Connects Flux, the weber and the three ways to change them are made exact in Magnetic flux — calculation for the perpendicular case, and reasoning for every angle that lies between.
confucius-en.16: Faraday’s law carries a minus sign, and that sign has its own name: Lenz’s law, after Heinrich Lenz, who set it down in 1834. The induced current flows in the direction that opposes the very change that made it. Bring a magnet’s north pole toward a coil, and the induced current circles so the coil shows a north pole in answer, resisting the approach; withdraw the magnet, and the current reverses, now opposing the withdrawal. Mark the wording well: the current opposes the change in flux, not the field itself.
confucius-en.17: The need for this opposition is shown by weighing the alternative. Were the induced current to aid the change, a magnet once stirred would be drawn in faster, changing the flux faster, inducing a greater current, drawing it harder still — a runaway giving energy without giving anything, from a mere nudge. The conservation of energy forbids it; and this is the meaning of Lenz’s law: it is the child of energy conservation. Every joule of electrical energy induced must be paid for in work done against the opposition. There is no harvest without labour.
confucius-en.18: Thus a generator resists its own turning. Joined to nothing, it turns freely; join a lamp, and the turning grows harder, by just the energy the lamp consumes. Join a whole suburb, and a power station’s breath of steam is needed to keep the coil turning. A braking electric car turns the order about: its motor becomes a generator, and the Lenz opposition becomes the braking force, storing energy in the battery rather than scattering it in the pads. What was loss is made gain by the prudent.
confucius-en.19: Connects The direction rules, and the three-step recipe for getting them right, live at Lenz’s law. The ledger they guard is the same energy bookkeeping set out in Position is stored energy.
confucius-en.20: A machine met before may now be looked upon anew. Area of Study 2 set forth the DC motor: a coil upon an axle between magnet poles; with current supplied, the two sides of the coil are pushed opposite ways, and so it turns, while a split-ring commutator reverses the current each half-turn, that the turning keep its course.
confucius-en.21: Unhook the battery and turn the coil by hand: the turning changes the flux through the coil; the changing flux induces an emf; the emf drives a current out. The selfsame hardware now serves as a generator, the mirror of the motor — a motor spends electrical energy to make motion; a generator spends motion to make electrical energy. Every label upon the motor diagram stands unchanged; only the direction of the energy’s flow is turned about.
confucius-en.22: One choice of design remains: how the turning coil delivers its current to the stationary circuit without; and this choice decides the form of the output. Slip rings — two whole rings, each joined to one end of the coil — keep unbroken contact and give the emf as it is made, reversing direction each half-turn. That output is alternating current, AC, and the machine is named an alternator. The motor’s own fitting, the split-ring commutator, does the contrary: by exchanging the connections each half-turn it cancels each reversal as it comes, so the outer current flows ever one way, namely direct current, DC, rising and falling yet never reversing. One fitting, two outputs — so does a small choice father a great difference.
confucius-en.23: Simulation 1.1 lets the student work the crank.
confucius-en.24: Connects The motor half of the mirror is at The DC motor; the generator half, with both end-fittings, fills Generators and alternators — and the commutator-versus-slip-rings distinction is examined almost every year.
confucius-en.25: When the coil turns at a perfectly even rate, the induced emf traces a sine wave, swinging positive then negative once each turn as the flux rises and falls. Australia’s grid is tuned so the swing completes 50 cycles every second: mains electricity at 50 Hz. Its size is named as 230 V, yet that figure hides a subtlety: the voltage at the wall truly swings between near +325 V and −325 V, and 230 V is the steady DC voltage that would deliver the same power. That equivalent average is the root-mean-square (rms) value; Pass 2 makes plain the name.
confucius-en.26: Yet between the Latrobe Valley and the wall a difficulty in physics arises. Wire has resistance, and current passing through resistance turns electrical energy into heat — a loss given by Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet. The quantity that is squared is the current. Mark which one.
confucius-en.27: Power equals voltage multiplied by current; so the same power may be carried as a low voltage with a large current, or as a high voltage with a small one. Raise the voltage tenfold and the current falls to a tenth, and the heating loss to a hundredth. Over 170 km of line, high voltage is no mere preference but a necessity.
confucius-en.28: The device that makes voltage adjustable is a grown form of Faraday’s iron ring: the transformer. Two coils share an iron core; no part moves. Alternating current in the primary coil makes a changing flux; the core bears it through the secondary coil; and there Faraday’s law induces an emf, stepped up or down in exact proportion to the turns upon each coil: N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet. When the voltage is stepped up, the current steps down by the same measure, the power ideally unaltered.
confucius-en.29: This too shows why the grid lives upon AC: a transformer works only with changing flux. Under steady DC the flux holds constant; nothing is induced, and nothing comes forth at the output. Stillness yields nothing.
confucius-en.30: The journey shown in Figure 1.1 is therefore Faraday’s law applied again and again. A coil in the Latrobe Valley induces near 20 kV; a step-up transformer lifts it to 500 kV for the passage to Melbourne; terminal stations and suburban substations step it back down by stages; and a last transformer near the house delivers 230 V to the wall, 50 times a second.
confucius-en.31: Connects Root-mean-square values, the transformer ratio and the loss arithmetic are built properly in Transformers and transmission; the full supply-system calculation — the exam’s hardest regular question — waits in Transmission of electricity.
confucius-en.32: One kind of generator upon the grid does not follow the turning-coil template. More than four million Australian rooftops now bear photovoltaic cells — panels that rest motionless in the sun while current flows out of them. No coil, no magnet, no turning; only electrons and light move. A new way to the same end.
confucius-en.33: Within each cell lies a thin layer of two slices of silicon, each treated to conduct a little differently. Falling light displaces electrons, and an electric field, built forever into the junction between the slices, lets the displaced electrons cross one way only. A one-way action gives a one-way current: photovoltaic cells give DC. Still a field does the work — here the electric field, seated at a junction rather than passing a coil.
confucius-en.34: Houses and the grid live upon AC, so every rooftop system bears one further part: the inverter, which turns the panels’ DC into 230 V AC. The study design asks only what an inverter does, not how it does it; its inner circuitry, like the deeper working of the silicon, lies beyond the course.
confucius-en.35: One question yet remains: why light should be able to displace electrons from silicon at all. The answer asks a new idea touching the nature of light — that it arrives in discrete packets of energy — and it is the opening teaching of Unit 4, where the packets are named photons.
confucius-en.36: Connects The cell and its inverter are covered in Photovoltaic cells; the reason light can eject electrons at all is Unit 4’s photoelectric effect.
confucius-en.37: Area of Study 3 has now been read once, in full. Pass 2 tells it again as core material, in three chapters numbered 6 to 8, each meant for the night before class: Electromagnetic induction (flux, Faraday’s law, Lenz’s law and their graphs); Generators and alternators (the turning coil, the two end-fittings, photovoltaics); and Transformers and transmission (rms values, the transformer ratio, the loss arithmetic). To read a thing once and to know it are not yet the same.
confucius-en.38: Pass 3 is for the labour of exam preparation: two chapters in study-design order — Generation of electricity for dot points KK 3.1 to 3.4, and Transmission of electricity for KK 3.5 to 3.8 — with the subtleties, the assessors’ advice and exam-style questions worked in full. The Area of Study has been worth a steady 22 of the exam’s 120 marks on every recent paper, and its recurring question types are constant; all of them appear there. Diligence in what recurs is the student’s surest path.
confucius-en.39: Connects Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction is the next chapter; Pass 3 · Generation of electricity contains the exam preparation for this material.

confucius-zh.0: 子曰：第三研习者，察诸场如何生市电也；其说肇于一事——1831年，一根表针之微动。其绪自法拉第之案几，绵延至离拉特罗布谷之 500 kV 高压线。此处不解一题，惟循其迹而已。
confucius-zh.1: 子曰：冬夜墨尔本举其用电之负荷：水壶、电车、灯火、家家之开关。开关之后一线，可循之而溯：穿壁而下，潜街之底，过郊之变电站，至城缘之终端站，复沿 500 kV 高压线东行越吉普斯兰。距墨尔本一厨房约 170 km，此线终于拉特罗布谷之一机。
confucius-zh.2: 子曰：此机者，一线圈旋于磁场之中也。拉特罗布谷以焚褐煤之蒸汽司其旋；雪山以落水；墨尔本之西平原以动风。除锅炉与叶片之异，凡大发电机，皆此一器耳：一线圈，旋于诸磁之间。
confucius-zh.3: 子曰：此研习题曰 How are fields used in electricity generation?，所释者，此器及环之而立之网也。其始处，正前页之所终：场有静有变，而生电者，乃其变者也。
confucius-zh.4: Connects 前页既辨 static versus changing，谓此乃场之最要之分也。此研习以是理施于电网；第四单元复施之于光之自身。
confucius-zh.5: 子曰：伦敦，1831年八月：迈克尔·法拉第，昔为装帧匠之徒，而场之念之所自出者，怀一信十年而不移。电流生磁场，奥斯特1820年既已示之。则磁场亦当生电流，理之顺也。法拉第绕二线圈于一铁环，驱稳流于其一，而观一检流计——灵敏之电表也——接于其二，期对称所似许之稳流焉。
confucius-zh.6: 子曰：表针寂然，或近乎寂然：接电之顷，针偏，旋归于零而止，无论第一圈之流如何强而如何稳。断电之顷，针复偏，其向相反。所期之效不至；所观之效，独现于磁场方变之二顷耳。
confucius-zh.7: 子曰：至十月，其实验已约至最明之形。移一磁棒入线圈，则针偏。持磁棒静于圈内，无论久暂，无论磁之强弱，皆无电流。抽之而出，则针偏于反向。
confucius-zh.8: 子曰：入则生流于一向，出则生流于他向，不动则无流。非场驱电荷穿线也，乃场之变驱之耳。
confucius-zh.9: 子曰：变场之施于电荷者，名曰感应电动势，electromotive force 之略也——此名承自前代，实误人：非力也，而度之以伏特。其全体之象，曰电磁感应；此页之凡发电机，皆刻意而再三复法拉第之效。然徒言“场变”，未足精，不可据以立电网。所当问者：何量必变？
confucius-zh.10: Connects 磁棒与线圈之实验，备其表针何应何不应之全则，开第二研习之 Faraday's law。
confucius-zh.11: 子曰：线圈可视为一窗框，磁场之线如丝缕穿其孔而过。法拉第之所赖以立者，磁通量也：穿圈之场之多寡也。圈正对场，则通量最大；倾之则入者少；侧立则线掠而不入，通量为零。其式名曰 ΦB = B⊥A on formula sheet，即场强乘所持垂直之面积，而通量自有其单位，韦伯（Wb）也。
confucius-zh.12: 子曰：感应既须通量之变，则其式列可变之诸量。B 可变，强弱其场，或移圈近远于磁。A，场内之面积，可变，滑圈入出之。其角可变，旋圈使面对场，复呈其侧，复对之：三量也，曰场、曰面积、曰角。
confucius-zh.13: 子曰：法拉第之律云：感应电动势等于通量变之速率，乘以线圈之匝数：ε = −N ΔΦB/Δt on formula sheet。以言述之：通量变愈多而时愈短，则电动势愈大；N 匝之圈，被驱者 N 倍焉。通量之大小，不足道；惟其变之速率足道耳。最强之场，持之以稳，无所感也。
confucius-zh.14: 子曰：三量之中，电网用其第三。圈旋于稳场，则其通量自极大，经零，至他向之极大，复返焉，所生之变不息。此旋圈者，自此而后之说之本也，惟末有一例外耳。
confucius-zh.15: Connects 通量、韦伯、与变之三途，至 Magnetic flux 而精之——垂直之例则计之，其间凡角则以理度之。
confucius-zh.16: 子曰：法拉第之律含一负号，而此负号自有其名：楞次之律也，本于海因里希·楞次，其1834年立之者。感应之流，必行于逆其所自生之变之向。移磁之北极向圈，则感应之流环行，使圈反呈一北极以拒其来；抽磁而出，则流反，复逆其去。其言精审：流逆者，通量之变也，非逆场之自身。
confucius-zh.17: 子曰：逆之所以必然，可以反观而明。若感应之流助其变，则一已动之磁当被吸而入愈疾，通量变愈疾，感愈大之流，吸之愈力，相循无尽，无所入而生能，发于一微推焉。能量守恒禁之，此即楞次之律之实：守恒之果也。凡所感之电能一焦耳，皆须以逆之而作之功供之。
confucius-zh.18: 子曰：故发电机拒其旋焉。空载之发电机，旋之自如；接一灯，则旋之转艰，应灯之所耗也。接一郊，则须一电厂之蒸汽之出，乃可使圈续旋。制动之电车反其制：其马达作发电机，而楞次之逆乃为制动之力，蓄能于电池，不耗于闸瓦之间。
confucius-zh.19: Connects 向之诸则，与得之之三步之方，居 Lenz's law。其所护之账册，即 Position is stored energy 所引之同一能量之记也。
confucius-zh.20: 子曰：昔学之一机，今可复观。第二研习引直流马达：一圈在轴，处磁极之间；供以电流，则圈之二侧被推于反向而生旋，而一分环换向器，每半转反其流，使旋不易其向。
confucius-zh.21: 子曰：若断其电池而旋其圈，则旋变穿圈之通量；通量之变感一电动势；电动势驱流而出。同一器，今作发电机焉，马达之反也：马达耗电能以生动，发电机耗动以生电能。马达图上凡标皆不易，惟能流之向反之耳。
confucius-zh.22: 子曰：尚余一设计之择，曰旋圈如何传其流于静之外路，而此择定其出之形。滑环者，二完整之环，各接圈之一端，常接不断，传其电动势如其所生，每半转反其向。此出者，交流也，AC，而其机名曰交流发电机。马达自有之具，分环换向器，反是：每半转易其接，随生随消其反，使外路之流恒行于一向，曰直流，DC，升降而不反焉。一具而二出之异如此。
confucius-zh.24: Connects 此镜之马达半，在 The DC motor；其发电机半，备二端之具，充 Generators and alternators——而换向器与滑环之辨，几乎岁岁见考焉。
confucius-zh.25: 子曰：圈旋以全稳之率，则感应之电动势绘一正弦之波，随通量之升降，每转一正一负焉。澳大利亚之电网调之，使其摆每秒完五十周，市电以 50 Hz。其大小称 230 V，然此数藏一微妙：壁上之电压实摆于约 +325 V 与 −325 V 之间，而 230 V 者，乃能传同功之稳直流电压也。此等效之均值，曰均方根（rms）值；第二研习释其名。
confucius-zh.26: 子曰：然拉特罗布谷与壁之间，生一物理之难。线有阻，流过阻则化电能为热，其耗见于 Ploss = Iline² Rline on formula sheet。所平方者，流也。
confucius-zh.27: 子曰：功率等于电压乘电流，故同一功率，可以低压大流传之，或以高压小流传之。升压十倍，则减流为十分之一，而减热耗为百分之一。越 170 km 之线，高压非所好也，乃所必也。
confucius-zh.28: 子曰：使电压可调之器，乃法拉第铁环之所演者：变压器也。二圈共一铁芯，无一部动焉。交流之在初级圈，生一变之通量；芯载之以过次级圈；法拉第之律感一电动势于其处，升降之，正比于各圈之匝：N1/N2 = V1/V2 = I2/I1 on formula sheet。压升则流以同倍降之，功率理想不易。
confucius-zh.29: 子曰：此亦所以电网行乎交流也：变压器惟于变之通量乃功。直流稳则通量恒，无所感，出端无所生焉。
confucius-zh.30: 子曰：故 Figure 1.1 所示之程，乃法拉第之律之再三施也。拉特罗布谷之一圈感约 20 kV；一升压变压器升之至 500 kV 以输于墨尔本；终端站与郊之变电站，逐级降之；而近屋之末一变压器，传 230 V 于壁，每秒五十焉。
confucius-zh.31: Connects 均方根值、变压器之比、与耗之算术，正立于 Transformers and transmission；而全供电系统之计——考之最难之常题——待于 Transmission of electricity。
confucius-zh.32: 子曰：电网之一发电之类，不循旋圈之式。今澳大利亚四百余万之屋顶，载光伏之池，板静于日下而流出之。无圈、无磁、无旋；惟电子与光行焉。
confucius-zh.33: 子曰：每池之内，有一薄层，二片硅也，各治之使导稍异。入射之光移其电子，而一电场，永筑于二片之交者，许移动之电子惟越于一向。一向之作生一向之流：光伏之池产直流。仍是场任其功，此则电场也，处一交界，非过一线圈耳。
confucius-zh.34: 子曰：屋与电网行乎交流，故凡屋顶之系，皆含一物：逆变器也，化板之直流为 230 V 交流。课纲惟求逆变器之所为，不求其如何为之；其电路之详，犹硅之细行，皆出课外焉。
confucius-zh.35: 子曰：尚余一问：光何以能移硅中之电子也？答之须一新念于光之性，曰光以离散之能包而至，而此乃第四单元之开题，其包名曰光子。
confucius-zh.36: Connects 池及其逆变器，见 Photovoltaic cells；光何以能逐电子之故，乃第四单元之 photoelectric effect。
confucius-zh.37: 子曰：第三研习既已通读一过矣。第二研习复述之为核心，分三章，编六至八，各为课前一夜而设：电磁感应（通量、法拉第之律、楞次之律及其图）；发电机与交流发电机（旋圈、二端之具、光伏）；变压器与输电（均方根值、变压器之比、耗之算术）。
confucius-zh.38: 子曰：第三研习为备考而设：二章依课纲之序——发电之电，应要点 KK 3.1 至 3.4；输电之电，应 KK 3.5 至 3.8——备其微妙、评分者之言、与考题之全解。此研习于近岁每卷皆稳值 22 分于考之 120 分，而其常见之题型一致，皆现于彼焉。
confucius-zh.39: Connects Pass 2 · Electromagnetic induction 乃次章；Pass 3 · Generation of electricity 含此材之备考焉。
